origin_explanation: from Ar. habba 'love', habib 'friend'; also in Tna. hababa, Te. (tä)hababa 'be kind'.

meaning: 'bind, tie, fetter'; 'ahbala, same; also causative of habala; habl (pl. 'ahbäl, 'ahbälät), (T) habl 'string, cord, rope, snare, (T) plait, spoils, booty (taken by snare), relationship' (from 'bind'); (habla rast 'rope serving to measure land, land that is measured by rope'); (habla kəsäd 'stole'; for the description see moṭäht); hablat, habläť 'plaited work, (T) ornamental collar for mules, (KG) anything pendent, hanging';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. habala 'tie with a rope, make a treaty', habl 'rope'; SAr. hbl 'conclude a pact', hbl 'alliance, pact', Soq. habəhol 'rope', Heb. ḥeḇel 'rope' (for Heb. ḥeḇel 'piece of field', from 'length of rope as unit of measure', SAr. hbl-t 'terraced field', cp. Amh. qäläd 'rope, piece of land measured by the qäläd-rope'), Aram.-Syr. ḥaḇlā 'rope', Md. habla, Ug. hbl, Akk. eblu. Schulthess 26 thinks that the verbs (Ar. habala, G. habala) are denominatives. ETHIOPIC: Te. habl 'rope', Tna. habli, Amh. habl. For G. habl 'relationship', cf. also Ar. (Dat) habl 'parents, tribe' (Landberg 1920:342), Te. habäl. See also habala II.

meaning: lie in wait, ensnare, (T) be dissolute, be lustful'; (MA 49 = Amh. bəlləʿəčč alä 'scintillate'; the Amharic translation of habala should be corrected into bəlləʿəṭ alä 'be cunning'); 'ahbala (K*), causative; tahabala 'act craftily, deal treacherously, act with deceit, ensnare'; habäli 'deceiver, deceitful, fraudulent, crafty'; habul 'deceitful, impostor, wicked, crafty, cheat, fraudulent, traitor'; also pass. part. of habala; habl, hǝbəl 'cunning, guile, deceit, ruse, snare, wickedness'; habil (Lt) 'fraud'; hablat (Gr.51) 'ruse, craft, guile'; tahablo, tahablot (Gr.404) 'fraud, deceit, treachery'; tahabäli 'deceiver, deceitful, fraudulent, presumptuous'; hababäli (form taken from Amharic; should read ḥabäbäli), (T.M) hababäli 'deceitful, deceiver, (T.M) lustful, lascivious';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. habala 'corrupt', but also habala 'set a trap', hibl 'shrewd, cunning', Heb. ḥāḇal 'act craftily against', Syr. ḥabbel, Akk. ḥabālu (with ḥ) 'tie, snare, harness, oppress, do wrong'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. habälä 'cheat', Te. habbälä 'twist', habäli 'bright, clever', Amh. abbälä 'coax, entice, tell lies, break one's promise', Har. ḥibäbäla, Gur. aba-bälä. This root is to be separated from Har. abäla 'refuse' against Cerulli 1936:230. For a possible connection between habl 'rope' (see habala I) and habala 'act craftily', that is 'to rope > twist', see Schulthess 28-9, Barth 1893:31, Noeldeke, ZDMG 54 (1900).161. See also hablaya.

meaning: 'act craftily, deceive, tell a lie, use guile, plot,
(MA) 'go away for good, vanish from sight'.

spoil, plunder, acquire by trickery, treat deceitfully, deal craftily, defraud, (T) lie, suspect, be distrustful'; tahablayā (Gr.5), causative; tahablayā, passive of hablayā; tahābalayā (K*) 'prey, booty, spoils'; hablet (Gr.51) 'act of plundering'.
origin_explanation: Probably denominative from habl (see habala II). ETHIOPIC: Tna. hablayā 'report what is said about someone'.

meaning: (T.MA.M) 'bedaub oneself, stain, contaminate, mix, intermingle, confound, make dirty, despise, (Gr.404) desecrate, (T.Y) strip off, (Sw. in D 1393,T) covet, be greedy'; tahabqaqa, tahabq°aq 'a', passive; also 'meddle, interfere, be dirty, be spoiled'; habquq 'mingled, stained, contaminated, polluted, dirty'; habqāqe, habq°āqe 'e' 'stain, spot, contamination, pollution, impurity, (Gr.404) desecration, (Sw. in D 103) blame'; habqaqi 'intemperance, inability to restrain one's desires'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: The connection with Syr. habak 'mix, mingle' suggested by Dillmann 103 is also taken up by Brockelmann 1928:210 who adds Ar. habaka 'weave well'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. habalaqlaqā 'mix' (from a root hbq with an inserted l); cp. also Har. habāqaqa 'dissolve by mixing with liquids'. Amh. abbaqāqā 'add flour to the batter in order to thicken it'. The root is related to Te. hablākā 'confound', and probably also Tna. hablāqā 'tie, twist'. Note that Md. hbqq 'embrace, cling to'. Ar. habaqaqa suggested by Dietrich 296 is to be connected with G. haqafa rather than with G. habqaqa. In G. habqaqa the last q is reduplicated. See also bāhqaqa, above.

meaning: (K*) 'ahābara 'scour, clean, polish, (T) color, paint'; also causative of hābara; hābāri (K*), act. part. of hābara; habāri, habarāt (K*) 'color, ink, complexion, beauty, appearance, kind, spot, scurf (which turns the skin white), skin rash'; (habra hamad 'ash-colored'; see hamad); (habra samāy 'sky-blue', 'azure'; see samāy); (habra q°ay 'of diverse colors'; see 'asq); (habra kabd 'purple'; see kabd); (habra sirā` [Lt] 'chess', so called because of colored pawns; see sirā`); habrāy (K*) 'vitriol blue'); habrāy masray (T) 'rose'; see sarayā); (za-habr 'speckled'); zazāhabr (Gr.51) 'of diverse colors, multicolored'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. habara 'be variegated (cloth)', hibr 'color, complexion, variegated cloth, ink' (considered by Fraenkel 247 an Aramaic loanword), Soq. hobir 'multicolored', Syr. habrā 'ink', Akk. ebru 'painted, made-up'; Heb. hbr (hifil) in Job 16:4 is translated 'be brilliant (with words)', as in Koehler 273; 'harangue' by Finkelstein (JBL 75[1956]329) on the basis of Akk. habāru 'be noisy, make noise'. On the numerous interpretations of this verb, see P. Dhorme, Le livre de Job (1926), p. 208, n. 4. ETHIOPIC: Te. habr 'color, shape', Tna. habri 'color'. Gur. habrā sāmāy, kabrā sāmāy 'azure', lit. 'color of sky', from Geez, with the unusual change of h to k. See also habarbare, habrata.

meaning: colored, of diverse colors, variegated, decorated with colors, dressed in a multicolored garment'; habārt, hubārt (fem. of habur) 'colored decoration, multicolored'.

(Lt) 'eruption on the skin'; habarbārā 'scar, bruise'; heb habārā 'bruise'; habārbārūt 'skin specks'; Aram. habbārūtā 'wound'; habārbārā 'bruise, sore', Akk. ibāru 'a mark or a discoloration of the skin'.
origin_explanation: See hābara, and hanbarbare.

meaning: make smooth, polish, scour, scrub, (Y) cleanse'; tahabrata, passive; habrāti 'polished, scoured, scrubbed, cleaned'; hubratāt, pl. of *hubrāt, (K*) 'multicolored decorations'.
(Y) 'imprison'; tahabbasa (T.Y), tahabsa (K*), passive; habs 'prison'; habūs 'imprisoned'; mahbūs (Lt) 'confined'.
origin_explanation: From Ar. habasa 'imprison'; also in Te. habbāsa 'take prisoner'. Tna. habbāsā passed into Cushitic: Bil. habās. See also hafs.

meaning: (T.M) 'serve, assist'; denominative from habit, habit; see habaya. Note the unusual form of the perfect.
origin_explanation: denominative from *habrat, from ahbara 'scour, polish'.

meaning: (yahbat) 'swell up, blow up, (T) become proud'; 'ahbata (Gr.52), causative; tahabata 'swell, swell (with pride)'; habāt, fem. habatt, for habāt-t (Gr.52) 'swell (L) ulcerous'; habāt, habāt 'boil, swelling, tumor, (K*)'; habātāt 'act of swelling, tumor'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. habita 'have a swollen stomach', hbt (hbt) 'become swollen'. Akk. ebēṭu, "etwa 'Unter Krämpfen anschwellen'" (von Soden). ETHIOPIC: Tna. habatā 'be swollen'. Te. habta, abbāṭā, Arg. abbāṭa, Gur. abbāṭā, Har. habāṭa.

meaning: (T) 'disregard, ignore, be reluctant to do something'.

(yahbi) (T.Y.M) 'give surety, give bail, be a bondsman, (T) guard'; 'ahbaya, (T) 'ahbaya 'offer security, give custody, receive in charge, (T) guard'; tahabaya, tahabbaya 'have charge of, take of, oversee, administer, assume the position of administrator and surety, sponsor, become surety, pledge oneself, stand as guarantor, vouch for, be a godparent'; (tahabayā 'godchild'); habāyi (pl. habāyi) 'chief, prefect, governor, (K*) warrantor'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. habā (hbw) 'grant favor, endow (with a gift), protect'. ETHIOPIC: Har. habi 'work, occupation, duty', Gur. epā (for ebbā) 'do, make, act'; also in Cushitic: Bil. abā 'guarantor'. Or. hūbi 'care'. Cohen 1931:182 suggests a Cushitic origin for Gur. ebbā, epā.
From Ar. hadid 'iron'; also in Te. haddad 'ironsmith'. Amharic hadid 'rail (from a railroad)' is likewise taken from Ar. hadid.

meaning: renew, restore, renovate, repair, (K*) inaugurate'; 'ahaddasa (K*), causative; tahaddasa, passive; 'astahaddasa 'renovate, restore, reestablish, inaugurate'; haddāsi, act. part. of haddasa; haddis (fem. haddis) 'new, recent, junior, the New Testament'; (hagg haddis 'the New Testament'); (haddis wabaluy 'Old and New Testaments').
'excellent'; haddāse 'renewal, restoration'; tahaddāsi, part. of tahaddasa; haddāso 'renewal'; tahaddāsi, part. of tahaddasa; tahaddāso (Y) 'renewal';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. haduṯa 'be new', SAR. h-hdt 'inaugu-rate', Heb. ḥiddēš 'renew', ḥādāš 'new', Aram. ḥad-dīt 'renew', ḥādāt 'new', Syr. ḥaddēṯ 'make new', Md. ḥdīṯ 'be new', Ph. ḥdš 'repair', Ug. ḥdṯ, Akk. edēšu 'be new'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. ḥaddāsa 'renew', Te. ḥaddāsa, Amh.Gaf. addāšā 'be new', Gur. ḥaddāšā 'renew, repair', Arg. ḥagōs 'new', Har. ḥagīs; passed into Cushitic: Bil. ḥaddas 'renew'.
metathesis in relation to dahaya.

meaning: = Amh. Sāsa, name of a tree which grows straight; its wood is preferred for making yokes; (note that Amh. Sāsa also means 'klipspringer');
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te.Tna. ḥada 'a tree'; also in Cushitic: Bil.Sa. ḥēdā.

meaning: title of the emperors of Ethiopia; ḥade-ge, same; qāla ḥade (Lt) 'the one who is in charge of transmitting the order of the king or the em-peror', lit. 'the voice of the emperor';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Dillmann 141 connects it with Ar. ḥazīya 'be esteemed, be an occupant of a high place'. Praetorius 1890:36 identifies it with SAR. ḥzy 'favor, good will of lords'. E. Cerulli, RSE 3 (1943). 276 derives it from ḥadāni 'tutor' (see hadana, below) with -āni > -āy > -ē. Conti Rossini 1912:171 derives it from Cushitic: Qem.Qua. asēna 'king' (Cushitic š going back to s < š). The identi-fication of ḥade with hadaya 'be engaged' (that is, the emperor being considered as someone engaged to his country), suggested by KWK 557, gives the impression of a folk etymology. ETHIOPIC: Tna. ḥaṣāy, Amh.Gur. aṣe, aṭe.

meaning: (T) 'shudder with fear, be shocked, feel ill at ease'.

'hold in the lap, nurse, nourish, suckle, feed, rear, bring up, train, educate, foster, take care of (one's young)'; tahadana, passive; aḥdāna, causative; also 'give a child a nurse'; taḥadna, passive; ḥadāni, act. part. of ḥadana; also 'tutor, guar-dian of children, educator'; ḥadānit (fem. of ḥadāni) 'nurse, wet nurse'; ḥadǝn (Gr.68), pass. part. of ḥadana; ḥǝdn, pl. ḥadān, (T) ḥǝṣn 'bosom, womb, lap, embrace, tutelage, guardianship, midst, middle, (T.KG) armpit, afterbirth'; ḥadān (pl. ḥǝdānāt) 'infant, young child, page'; ḥadnat 'nursing, nourishing, education, tute-lage, upbringing'; ḥadānāwi (Lt) 'childish'; ḥadānǝnnā, ḥǝdānǝnnā 'childhood'; ḥadīnot (Lt) 'charge'; māḥǝdān (pl. maḥādǝn, māḥǝdānāt) 'bo-som, womb, mother's lap, uterus'; taḥadīnot (Lt) 'charge';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. ḥidn 'breast, bosom', ḥadana 'bring up, raise', Soq. danaḥ 'breast' (with metathesis), ḥoṣen 'bosom', Aram.-Syr. ḥannā 'lap' (< *ḥanā < ḥadnā), Md. ḥana, Akk. ḥaṣānu 'embrace' (vo-den), 'shelter' (CAD). ETHIOPIC: Tna. ḥaṣānā 'protect, adopt', Amh. aḥ-ṭānā 'raise a child'; 'lap': Te. ḥəṣn, Tna. ḥəṣnī; 'child': Te.Tna. ḥəṣan, Amh. ḥəṣan. For Amh. Gaf. ǝn 'thigh', Har. ǝn 'waist', see Leslau 1979. 183. The root passed into Cushitic: Bil. ḥatān 'boy, servant', Sa. ḥəṣan. See also mḥdn.

meaning: (L) ḥaṣaya 'betroth, affiance, be engaged (to marry), give the dowry (the boy's or the girl's parents)'; ʾaḥdaya 'give a daughter in marriage, order to give a dowry'; taḥadaya 'be betrothed, be given the dowry'; taḥadāya 'be engaged to each other'; ḥadāyi (K*), ḥaṣāyi (Gr.68), act. part. of ha-daya; ḥaduy (K*) 'engaged (man)'; ḥadāyt, ḥadīt 'engaged (woman)'; ḥadē (T) 'betrothal, dowry'; ḥadayat 'act of betrothing, state of being betrothed';

(T) 'connection with Heb. ḥāšā (ḥsy) and Ar. ḥaṣṣa (ḥsy) suggested by Appleyard 8 is convincing since Heb. ḥāšā means 'divide' and 'share'. As for Arabic, a corresponding root would be ḥzy or ḥdy in view of G. ḥdy.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. ḥaṣāyā 'betroth, affiance', Te. (ḥsy), Amh. aččā, Gaf. ačā; passed into Cushitic: Bil. ḥačay, Qem. ičonā 'bride'.
ETHIOPIC: Te. ḥafa 'sharp curved knife', Amh. 'short sword'. See also ʾafā.

meaning: (T.K.M) 'take earth or grain with two hands held cupped together'; ʾaḥfana (DTW 947), causative; taḥafna (DTW 947), passive; ḥafn 'handful, fistful, (Lt) fist';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar.ḥafna 'handful', Šh. ḥfun 'scoop up the cupped hands', Heb. ḥopen 'the hollow of the hand', Aram.-Syr. ḥupnā, Md. ḥupna, Ug. ḥpn 'ta-fistful' (Caquot 472), Akk. upnu 'handful'. ETHIOPIC: Te. ḥafən 'both hands full', ḥaffāna 'take with both hands', Tna. ḥafnī 'handful', Amh. affǝnnī; passed into Cushitic: Aw. afān-x"a 'ta-handful'.

meaning: 'prison'; (bəta ḥafs) 'prison'; ḥafsot (K*) 'lack of freedom, lack of liberty';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. ḥafs 'prison', from ḥabs; see ḥab-sa.

meaning: 'rake up, sweep up, scatter chaff, carry away (specifically an amount held in two hands), take a handful'; ʾaḥfaša (DTW 947), causative; taḥafaša (DTW 947), passive; ḥafāši (Gr.68), act. part. of ḥafaša; ḥafūš (Gr.68), pass. part. of ḥafaša; ḥafs, (T) ḥafs 'amount carried away in two hands, handful, gift placed in the hands of someone'; ḥafšāt (Gr.69) 'sweeping (n.), carrying away';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. ḥafaša 'gather, collect', Šh. ḥfeš, Heb. ḥāpaš 'search', PBh. ḥāpaš 'dig, search', Aram. ḥā-paš 'dig', Ug. ḥpš-t 'straw picker', von Soden 223 compares Akk. epēšu 'make, do'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. ḥafāšā, ḥafāšā 'scoop up', Te. ḥafša 'scrape corn together', Har. ḥafāša 'take something with one or two hands or with an instrument', Arg.

meaning: Amh. affaṣā, Gur. affaṣā; passed into Cushitic: Qab.Al. ḥamfaṣo, Sa. ḥafaṣ 'take hold of'.

origin_explanation: see ḥanfafa I.

meaning: 'cause to abandon, cause to send away';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. ḥagafa 'oppose and repel, confront an adversary in a duel', iḥtaǧafa 'turn someone away from something'. In Arabic the verb seems to be a denominative from ḥagafa 'shield' (see ḥagafā, be-low).
SEMITIC: Ar. ḥagafa 'shield made of skins'. See also ḥagafa, above.

meaning: 'lay down laws, write laws, legislate, ordain, decree, institute laws'; taḥaggaga, passive; also (Lt) 'accept the laws'; ḥagg (pl. ḥagag, ḥagagāt) 'law, decree, canon, rite, rule, regulation, statute, ordinance, norm, covenant, usage, custom'; (za-ḥagg 'lawful'); (za-ʾanbala ḥagg 'unlawful'); (ba-ḥagg 'lawfully, legally'); (šarāʾe ḥagg 'lawgiver'); (ṣaḥāfe ḥagg 'lawyer'); (ḥagga [T.K] 'like, as, just as, according to'); ḥaggāgi 'lawgiver, legislator'; ḥaggag (K*), pass. part. of ḥaggaga; ḥaggāwi 'lawful, legal, legitimate';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. ḥaǧǧa 'plead in a lawsuit', ḥuǧǧa 'argu-ment, plea', Sar. ḥg 'ordinance, right, statute', relat-ed to ḥqq (see ḥaqqa, below). ETHIOPIC: Tna. ḥaggā 'law', ḥaggāgā 'lay down laws', Te. ḥaggā 'be fixed, be sure', ḥaggāgā 'delim-it', Amh. ḥagg 'law' (from Geez), Amh. ḥaggāgā 'pass laws', Gur. ḥagg 'law' (from Amh.); passed into Cushitic: Bil. ḥagge 'law'.

meaning: (K*) 'tie, bind', hagəl (T.M) hagəl 'bond, fetter, shackle', (KG) = Amh. manəqəqo 'rods of the yoke which press against the oxen's necks to keep the yoke from sliding off';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. hiğl 'fetter, chain, anklet', Sar. m-hğl 'enclosed field', Soq. hğl 'surround', Sh. hagal 'anklet ring', Aram. hagal 'encircle', Syr. hgal 'en circle', Gird. 'The connection with hgr suggested by Buhl 214 is possible: Ar. hağğara 'make an enclo sure', Sar. hgr 'reserve' (that is, 'enclose, set apart'), Heb. hāgar 'gird, encircle', Aram. hāgar, Syr. hāgar, Ug. hgr 'gird'. For this root, see Marrassigni 89ff. ETHIOPIC: Te. hagl 'bracelet for the foot', from Ar. hagl.
see also hangaz.

tahagaza (D.T), tahagza (K*) 'be helped, make firm for oneself (e.g. ship by dropping the anchor), make fast for oneself'; tahagaza (T.H.K*) 'help one another'; SEMITIC: Ar. hagaza 'intervene' which could be developed into 'help'. ETHIOPIC: Te. hagza 'be an ally'. Tna. hagğäza 'help', Amh. aggäzä; passed into Cushitic: Bil. hag- gada 'repulse, prevent, protect' (with alterna d. z), Sa. hagaz.
from Arabic: hağama 'cup'; also in Tna. hag™äma 'draw blood with a cup', Te. hagəma, Har. hagäma, Amh. aggämä; passed into Cushitic: Sa. hagam.

Probably from Ar. hağar 'stone' (also Te. hagrat 'a sort of red stone or red earth') rather than from an original meaning 'compact, solid' from a non-at tested root hgr, as suggested by Dillmann 130 and Marrassigni 91. Note the structure zabiba hag°ər instead of the expected hag°ära zabib, unless hag°ər has a special meaning.

meaning: 'lizard, also translated 'crocodile'. Reinisch 1902:187 compares it with Som. ğahas 'crocodile'. See also hargas.
origin_explanation: Reinisch 1902:187 compares it with Som. ğahas 'crocodile'. See also hargas.

hagayäwi (yəhgi) 'spend the summer, become summer (denominative)'; hagayäwi 'pertaining to summer'; ETHIOPIC: Te. Tna. hagay 'dry season', Amh. hagay, agay; also in Cushitic: Bil.Sa.Be. hagäy, Kam. hagü.
hakim 'physician, philosopher, wise man'; from Arabic: hakkama 'practice medicine', hakim 'physician'; also in Te. hakim 'physician', Amh. hakim.

tahlaba (Gr.42), causative; tahlaba (Gr.42), tahlaba (K*), passive; haläbi (K*Gr.42) 'who milks'; halib 'milk, curds, juice, milky juice'; halub (K*Gr.42) 'milked'; halbat (K*TS) 'act of milking, state of being milked'; halab (T) halob 'sour milk'; mähaläb, pl. mähaläb (K*) 'jar for milk'; SEMITIC: Ar. halaba 'milk' (v.), halab 'fresh milk', Soq. heləb 'milk' (v.), Heb. hālāb 'milk' (n.), hālāb 'milk' (v.), Aram. hālāb 'milk' (n.), Syr. halba, Pun. hlb, Md. halba, Ug. hlb, Akk. halābu 'milk' (v.). ETHIOPIC: Tna. haläbä 'milk' (v.), Te. halba, Arg. haläba, Har. haläba, Amh. alläbä, Gur. aläbä. Te.Tna. halib 'milk' (n.), Arg. hayu, Har. hay (< hlb), Gur. eb, Amh.Arg. ayb 'cheese' (< hlb).
ETHIOPIC: Amh. alläbä, same.
personification of the feminine proper name Oliba, Heb. oholībā (Ez. 23:4) who committed adultery.
ETHIOPIC: perhaps Te. halbat 'nose rope for the camel' > 'nose'.

meaning: 'Lenten dish consisting of barley, pepper, and beans';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. hulba 'kind of food (called fariqa) which is given to women when pregnant', Ar. (Yem) hulba, hulba 'vegetable stew prepared with fenugreek'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. halbat 'stew of barley, pepper, and so on', Amh. albät, halbat.

meaning: 'intermingle, be mixed'; (the meaning 'place, make enter' given by M on the authority of T.Y is not found in their dictionaries and is probably to be identified with hallada I).

origin_explanation: tahalafa 'swear, curse, execrate, (K*) swear to one another, (T.M) speak to one another' (should be tahalafa); note that while Dillmann 70 records it as halafa (with h), the verb is halafa (with h) in the quoted passages; tahalafa (K*) 'swear to one another'; 'astahalafa (T) 'proclaim, speak'; tahläf (K*) 'oath'; tahläf (Gr.43) 'imprecation'; SEMITIC: Ar. halafa 'swear', SAr. hlf, Mh. h-ḥalef. See also hlf, below.

tahläf (T) 'of necessity, perforce'; comes perhaps close to Ar. halafa 'swear' (for which see halafa, above), and also half 'the act of confed erating, the act of making a compact or confedera cy' (Lane), halafa-hu 'he held fast to it'.
probably a repetition of the preceding 'amḥala in Lifschitz 162.

meaning: 'burn, be burning, be scorched, (T.Y) dry up';
origin_explanation: hallala, see 'ahlala, below. 'ahlala, (T.Gr.2) 'ahlala 'char, scorch, burn (tr), dry up'; tahalla (Gr.41) 'be burned, be scorched'; halläle 'burn (n.), burning' (n.); həlul (TS.Gr.41) 'burnt, that burns'; 'ahläli (Gr.41) 'perfumer'; mahallal, mahläl 'perfumer (who extracts the perfume by heating the spices), dealer in un guents'; mahläl (Gr.41) 'place where one burns, means of burning'; ETHIOPIC: Te. halläla 'be burning', Tna. hallä 'blaze', Amh. hallälä 'dry out, become dry'; probably also Amh. allälä 'be in heat (animal)'; passed into Cushitic: Qem. aläl 'be in heat', Sa. halal 'burn'. See also 'allala, halale.

meaning: 'itch, scratch, scrape, have an itch, (T.M) be cut into, be notched';
origin_explanation: tahakka, tahakakä, passive; hakäki (K*), act. part. of hakaka; hakuk (K*), pass. part. of hakaka; hakak, hakak 'itch' (n.); hakak 'pointed stick'; SEMITIC: Ar. hakka 'scratch', Mh. hek, Heb. (from hkk) 'fish hook', PbH. hākaķ 'scratch', hakak, Syr. hak (from hkk), Md. hkk (huk) Akk. ekēku. ETHIOPIC: Hkk and hnkk (augmented with n): hakäkā, hank™äk™ä 'scratch', Te. hakka, hankäka 'scratch, scrape', Har. hakäka, Arg. hakkäka, Amh. akkaka, Gur. akäka, Gaf. ank™ak™a 'scab'; also in Sa.Af. hakak 'itch, scratch', Bil. hank™ak™. Had. hakakä-kko.

meaning: 'load, put a load on a pack animal, place the pack saddle'.

origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. hakama 'restrain'. ETHIOPIC: Te. hakma 'decide, rule'; from the Semitic root hkm 'be wise'.

meaning: 'be gathered, come in'; məhlal (T, should read məhlāl, məhlāl), məhlal (Sw.in D 1394) 'place where people gather'; 'eat, SAR. hll 'encamp''
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. hallala 'take up residence', hallala 'make alight at', SAR. hll 'encamp'.

meaning: 'dream'; 'ahlama (K*.Gr.41) 'cause to dream'; tahallama, (K*.Gr.41) tahalma 'be dreamed, dream, see visions'; 'astahalama (Gr.41) 'cause to dream often'; halāmi 'one who dreams, dreamer'; həlum (Gr.41) 'dreamed'; halmat (Gr.41) 'act of dreaming'; 'ahlāmi, mahlami 'that causes to dream'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. halama 'dream' (v.), SAR. hlm (n.), Šh. helm, Heb. hālām 'dream' (v.), Aram. hālam, Syr. hālam, Ph. Ug. hlm, Md. hilma 'dream' (n.). ETHIOPIC: Te. halma 'dream' (v.), Tna. halāmā, Amh. allāmā.
seems to be a composite noun; also Amh. halmelmelek, same from Geez).

meaning: 'be sick' (the form is strange); helpāpā, (T) helpāpā, əlpāpu 'sickness, disease';
origin_explanation: see also ənppāpe.

meaning: 'become round, grow round'; mahalaq (T.K.M) 'round, circular, (D) circular table'; həlaqat (pl. vəhlāq, həlaqāt, həlaqātāt) 'ring, finger ring, (L) circle, a circular figure (ring, necklace)';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. halqa 'ring of metal', hallaqa 'draw a circle' (denominative), tahallaqa 'sit in a circle', Soq. halqeh 'ring'. ETHIOPIC: Te. halqāt 'hair ring'.
perhaps for halqa and metathesis of lahqa (for which see below).

SEMITIC: Ar. halq 'throat', hulqūm 'larynx' (see Landberg 1920:475-6), Šh. halqūt 'Adam's apple', malqohayim 'palate' (metathesis of hlq; see 25), Ug. hlq-m 'throat, neck' (Gordon 397), Ak. pi, lāq pi (Holma 25, von Soden 555). ETHIOPIC: Amh. allāqt 'depression at the base of the neck directly below the Adam's apple'; Te. halqəm 'throat', from Ar. hulqūm; passed into Cushitic: Bil. halqum.
from Gk. holkē 'drachm as a weight'; also in halqi. See also halqat I.
see also halqat II.
see halq II.
there seems to be a connection with halāq.

see Hommel 1877:8, 38, 54, 92. Dillmann 67 suggests Ar. halis, hilāsiyy 'of mixed colors, hair having whiteness mixed with its blackness'; note also hilā-siyy 'a domestic fowl begotten between an Indian and a Persian fowl' (Lane). See also 'alastayo.

SEMITIC: either related to Ar. halaṣa (with h) 'be pure, be free from admixture', or G. halaṣa is another spelling of hallaṣa and in this case the comparison with Ar. halaṣa is more evident. ETHIOPIC: Amh. allātā 'be bland', alləčča 'stew vegetables but not containing bärbärre-pepper'.
seems to be a transcription of Heb. hālaṣ.
transcription of Heb. heleṣ.
SEMITIC: Ar. halata 'shave (the head), pluck (wool)'.

meaning: 'bribe, give a bribe, corrupt with a bribe, (T) accept bail'; tahallaya, (K*) tahalya 'be bribed, accept bribes, become corrupt by bribes'; halyān; see below.
origin_explanation: either denominative from halyān, below, or is related to Ar. hala'a 'give money to' or to halā (h) 'give something as gratuity', SAR. hl 'pay a sin offering' (Müller).

meaning: 'sing, celebrate with songs'; 'ahlaya (Lt), causative; tahalya 'be sung, sing for oneself'; halāyi (fem. halāyt) 'who sings, singer, minstrel'; halle 'song' (perhaps for halle 'hallelujah'; Dillmann 69); halyat 'act of singing, canticle'; māhalat (pl. mahālay, māhaletāt) 'song, canticle, hymn, psaltery, musical instrument, service in which dagg"a (see above) is used'; (māhaleta śage 'Canticle of the Flower', a collection of hymns in verse composed in honor of the Virgin); (nəwāya māhalet 'musical instrument'); (qəne māhalet 'the western part of the outer corridor of the Ethiopian church', lit. 'place where hymns are sung'); mahālay 'Song of Songs'; mahālay (K*) 'place where one sings'; māhaletāwi 'musician, singer, troubadour, the dābtara (see above) who spends time in the qəne māhalet (see above)';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. halā (hlw) 'be sweet, be delicious', haliya 'be agreeable', muhalli 'musician' (Dozy), Heb. māhalat, technical musical term (possibly from the root hly, see Baumgartner 540), Ar. halī, hālā 'be sweet', Syr. holī, Md. hla. ETHIOPIC: Tna. halāyā 'chant', Te. hala, Amh. hallāyā 'sing' (from Geez), māhalet 'sacred song' (from Geez); passed into Cushitic: Bil. halay 'sing'.

meaning: 'bribe, gratuity, gift, reward'; halyāna (T) 'give a bribe' (denominative);
origin_explanation: from Ar. hulwān 'gratuity'; see hly, above.

ETHIOPIC: Tna. hanzazā 'produce a lot of fruit' (Wajnberg 1937c: 201), with alternance of liquids. The verb could also be considered a denominative from halziz, below, with the meaning 'bring forth a shellfish'.
from Ar. halazūn 'snail'. See also halzaza.

ETHIOPIC: Te. hambalāy, hambalāy 'gray horse', Tna. 'ambalāy 'white horse', Amh. ambālay. Praetorius 1879:166 derives Amh. ambālay 'white horse' (and this would apply to the other languages) from Amh. ambāl 'breast plate for a horse, saddle', ambālay meaning originally 'that with a saddle', that is, 'horse', specializing into the meaning 'white horse'. This derivation is not convincing. Praetorius, ZDMG 64 (1910), 621 states that hambalay is an Amharic loanword. The root passed into Cushitic: Bil. hambalāy.

meaning: 'ashes, dust'; hamada, hamada (yahməd) 'become ashes, be extinguished' (denominative); 'ahmāda (K*), causative; tahamada, tahamada 'be burnt to ashes, become ashes'; həmūd 'burnt to ashes, reduced to ashes'; hamadāwi 'ash-colored';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. (Dat) hamad 'be hot, burn', and perhaps also Ar. hamada (h as against h of Geez) 'the fire subsided but its embers remained unextinguish-

meaning: 'snow, hoarfrost, frost, (T.Sw. in D 79) damage to grain because of frost'.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te.Tna. Har. hamādā 'ashes', Arg. hamādā, Amh. Gur. amād; Amh. ammādā 'reduce to ashes'.

meaning: Amh. amādāmado 'a kind of plant which has whitish leaves'.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. amhād kābo 'having white dots or spots on it', from hamad 'ashes, dust'.

SEMITIC: Ar. hamiga 'spoil, decay, be corrupt', Ar. (Dat) hamig 'become turbid'. Note Ar.hmg (with h) as against G. hmg (with h). ETHIOPIC: Te. haməgā 'make muddy', Tna. hamrā-gā (with augmented r), Amh. (t)ammāgā 'be moldy, smell bad'.

meaning: 'gather, collect, enclose cattle in a pen'; tahaməgā, passive. hamug, pass. part. of haməgā; haməgā 'impunity, dimness (of sight), haziness'; hamgat 'act of being turbid, turbid state, act of causing to be turbid, dimness, muddiness'.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. ammāgā 'gather, collect'.
SEMITIC: Ar. (Yem) habhab 'melon' (Müller). Fisher 86 compares Ug. hm-t 'bottle (for wine)'.
ETHIOPIC: Te.Tna. hamham 'gourd', Amh. hamham (from Geez); also in Cushitic: Bil.Sa.Af. hamham.

ETHIOPIC: Enlarged from the root hml (see hamala I). Te. hamalāmala 'become brown', Tna. hamalāməl 'brown', hamalāməlay 'not very dark', Amh. hamal, hamalāməl 'green, verdant' (from Geez); passed into Cushitic: Bil. hamalāməl.
From Ar. hamala 'carry'; also Amh. hammalā. Te. hammal 'one who carries anything'.
From Ar. hamal; also Amh. amāl.

lit. 'the month of green (cereals)', from haml (see hamala I); so also Conti Rossini 1923:376. For transfer of meanings, cp. Burgi yebala 'a green cereal', and the name of month when crop is cut. ETHIOPIC: Te. hamle, Tna. hamlā, hamle, Amh. hamle.

From Aram.-Syr. hāmilā, hāmilta 'a rough outer garment' (Noeldeke 1910:39); related to haml, hamla 'garment of wool, fringes of a carpet' (see on this noun Vollers 1896:630). For its connection with melāt, see Noeldeke 1910:39. ETHIOPIC: Amh. hamelāt 'headcloth' (from Geez).

meaning: 'be in pain, suffer illness, be ill, have labor pains, be afflicted, suffer distress, (K) have a fever'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: From the root hmm 'be hot'. Ar. hamma 'heat', humma 'have a fever', Ar. (Dat) hmy 'be hot', SAr. hmm 'hot season' (?), Sh. hmm 'be hot', Heb. ham (hmm) 'be warm, be hot', Aram. hāmam, Syr. ham, Ug. hm 'heat', Akk. emēmu 'be warm, be hot'. For the relation between 'be hot, be feverish' and 'be sick' in Indo-European, see Buck 302. ETHIOPIC: Tna. hamamā 'be sick, suffer pain', Te. hamma, Amh. ammāmā 'be painful, be sore', amāmā-w 'he felt sick'. For Geez hamāmāt 'Passion (of Christ), Good Friday', cp. Tna. hamamat, Amh. hamamat, amat, Gur. amamat. For G. astahāmāma 'care for the sick, nurse', cp. Ar. dāwā (dwy) 'nurse' and dawā (dwy) 'be sick'.

meaning: 'be envious, be jealous, (K) be stingy, be avaricious'.

'be humiliated, be despised, be bad, be overcome, be stupid, be foolish' (M quoting T.Y translated hamaqa also 'coprire di un velo'; he apparently mistook Amh. tāsānnāfa 'be overcome' [given by T.Y as the translation of G. hamaqa] for tāsāffānā 'he covered'); 'ahmaqa (K*), causative; 'astahamaqa (D.K) 'consider stupid, (K) consider disgraceful, consider despicable, (T) despise, esteem lightly'; hamuq (D.K)'silly, foolish, (T.K) despised, unimportant, wretched'; hamaq (D.K) 'foolishness, (T.K) humiliation'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. hamiqa 'be foolish, be stupid'. ETHIOPIC: Te. hamqa 'become weak', Tna. hamāgā 'become bad, be spoiled, lose its value'.
for tahramt, see harama.

SEMITIC: Dillmann 75 connects the root of hamar 'ship' with that of hāmōr 'donkey' from a root hmr 'carry' (so also Ullendorff 1968:127). While it is true that Aram. hmr has the meaning 'load' and that hmr can be identified with hml 'carry' (with alternance of liquids), neither hmr nor hml exists in Geez. Note that G. hamala 'carry' is an Arabic loanword. ETHIOPIC: Tna. hamār 'ship', Amh. hamār 'ship, ark' (from Geez).

meaning: 'red berry'; hamara sārā, hōmara sārā, hōmara sārā, (KG) hamara sār 'pods, husks (that swine eat)'. Ludolf 31 renders it 'Greek bean', identifying sārā with Gk 'Greece'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. 'ahmar 'red', Sar. hmr-t 'red' (?), PbH. hāmarmar 'become red' (figurative meaning from 'be hot, be parched'), Akk. emēru 'become red'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. hamār 'red', Te. hamār 'red-brown', Amh. hamār 'reddish (horse)', hamra-wi, hamray 'pink, purple' is probably an Arabic loanword. See also homar I.
from Ar. humār (for which see Landberg 1920:482); also in Te. homār 'tamarind', Tna. hamār, homār, homar, Amh. homār; also in Cushitic: Som. hamar, homar.
it is tempting to connect it with hamar II 'red berry', but if it had the meaning of 'red' Tayyā would not have translated it by 'kind of color'.

meaning: = Amh. wāgān translated by M 'side, kin, family, tribe, race'. Tayyā's translation is doubtful; one has the impression that the translation is not complete.
'acorn'; homara sārā (D. under hamar, Gr.45) 'carob tree, carob fruit', (T) = Amh. qamma 'a tree whose fruit grows in bunches and is edible when cooked'. See also hamar II.

origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. hammāsa 'swim', Tna. hamāsā, hambāsā (with augmented b, due to m?).

meaning: 'lick salt, eat bitter and salty plants'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Dillmann 1856:167 connects it with Ar. hamada 'graze upon acid plants', but the correspond ence s:d is not normal.

meaning: 'dry grain, parch grain lightly to facilitate grinding'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. hamasa 'fry meat', Ar. (South Ar) hamas 'roast coffee'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. hammāsā 'parch grain', Te. hammāsa. The root also occurs in Cushitic: Amh. bīmūs 'Durrakorn das nicht zur Reife gelangen kann weil vom Reife versengt oder in Folge von Wurmstich' (definition given by Reinisch 1887:31).

SEMITIC: Mb. hamt 'lower belly, pubes', Heb. homes 'belly', Akk. emšu; for Syr. humšā, see R. Degen (OC 62[1978].53-9) who considers it a Hebrew loanword because on the basis of Hars. hamt, one would expect t in Syriac. See on this root W. Müller, Der Islam 57 (1980). 57. ETHIOPIC: Te. hams 'pubes, abdomen', Tna. hams, Amh. ams 'vagina'; passed into Cushitic: Bil. himz 'genitals'. The connection with Amh. antāt 'intestine, bowels' suggested by Reinisch 1887:36 and Guidi 469 is doubtful.

SEMITIC: The root of the various languages is yhm, hwm, or hmw. Arabic huma, humma 'venom', Heb. hemā 'heat, poison, rage', Aram. hāmā 'anger', hemjā 'heat, venom, rage', Md. himta 'venom, anger', Ug. hmt 'venom', Akk. imtu. ETHIOPIC: Tna. hamot 'bile', Amh.Gur. amot, Gaf. amotā; transferred meaning in Te. hamot 'pride', Tna. hamot 'courage'. Amh. amot; passed into Cushitic Kham. hamut 'bile', Aw. amut.

meaning: 'soot, charcoal, black liquid, blackness'; (māya hammat 'ink', lit. 'liquid of soot'); hammata (T.MA. Sw. in D 1393) 'cause to (denominate)'; tahammata (DTW 939) 'burn, become charcoal'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. hamma (hmm) 'become black', hummām 'charcoal, ashes', Ar. (Dat) humum 'soot', Sh. hum(m) 'charcoal', perhaps also Heb. hūm 'black and white'. ETHIOPIC: Te. (tā)hamāma 'become sooty', hammāt 'soot', Tna. hammāt 'coal', hammātā 'become charcoal' (denominative), Amh. ammāt 'soot, ash from burning incense (used for healing)', ammātā 'reduce to ashes' (denominate).

meaning: 'become a son-in-law, acquire a son-in-law, contract marriage, marry, become a kinsman' (denominate); tahamāwa 'be related by marriage'

phonetics: 'astahāwama (T), causative of tahamawa; ham (pl. 'ahmāw) 'father-in-law, son-in-law'; the suffix pronouns are attached to the base hamu-; hamāt 'mother-in-law, daughter-in-law'; hamayt (T) 'in-laws'; 'astahāwamaw 'who is a near relation, who is a kinsman'; tāhmā 'affinity, in-law relationship, sons-in-law'.
meaning: 'father-in-law, son-in-law'; the suffix pronouns are attached to the base hamu-; hamāt 'mother-in-law, daughter-in-law'; hamayt (T) 'in-laws'; 'astahāwamaw 'who is a near relation, who is a kinsman'; tāhmā 'affinity, in-law relationship, sons-in-law'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. ham 'father-in-law', Sh. him 'brother-in-law', Heb. hām 'father-in-law', Aram. hāmā, Syr. hāmā, Md. ham, Akk. emu. ETHIOPIC: Te. ham 'father-in-law, son-in-law', Tna. hamu, Arg. hamāč, Har. hamāč, Amh.Gur. amat, Gaf. amā; for 'mother-in-law', cp. Te.Tna. hamat, Amh.Gur. amat, Har. hamāt.

meaning: 'speak ill against someone, disparage, revile, calumniate, blame, slander, defame, bring an accusation, curse'; 'ahmaya (K*), causative; tahamya, passive; tahāmaya, reciprocal of hamaya; also 'treat with offense, disgrace'; 'astahāmaya (Lt), caus. of tahāmaya; tahamyo 'reviling, slandering'; hamāy, hamāyi (pl. hamayt), act. part. of hamaya; hamuy, pass. part. of hamaya; also 'disgraceful, infamous, detestable, shocking'; hamet, (K*) homet, (D 1427) hamyat 'reviling, calumny, abuse, slander, invective, blame, suspicion of falsehood'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Perhaps Ar. hamiya 'alā 'be angered'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. hamāyā 'slander', Te. hama (hmy), Arg. hamma, Har. hema, hēmāwa, Amh. amma, Gur. ama; perhaps passed into Cushitic: Had. hamā-kko, Som. ham, Or. hammu, Sa. hammi. Cerulli 1936:418 considers it a common root with Semitic.

meaning: 'poison, venom, bitterness, rage, furor, wrath, gall, (Lt) scorpion'; hamaza (T.K*) 'be poison, to poison, be enraged' (denominate); hamzāwi (Gr.45) 'poisonous'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. hamz 'pungency, acridity'.
from Ar. hannā 'tenderness of heart' (Müller), or could it be a transferred meaning from hannā, the mother of Mary?

phonetics: henā (KG I, but not KG II, III)

meaning: 'reddish-orange cosmetic gained from the leaves of the henna plant)';
origin_explanation: From Ar. hinnā; also in Syr. hennā, Te. henna, Har. hinna, Amh. hinna.

meaning: 'put forth berries, bear fruit, bear grapes, sprout'; hanbub 'that has berries'; hanbāb 'berry, bunch of grapes, grain'; (hanbāba māy 'drops of water'); (hanbāba balas 'fig');
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: from hbb with augmented n: Ar. habba 'berry', Sar. hbb 'cereal crop'. ETHIOPIC: Te. habb-āt 'berry', Tna. habbi, in habbi koso 'anthelmintic', lit. 'the berry of the koso-tree', habbi 'anthelmintic, tapeworm'; Tna.Amh. abāsuda 'cumin', from Ar. habba sawdā 'black grain'.

meaning: 'breastplate for a horse, saddle, packsaddle, saddle blanket, (T) wooden headrest (in the shape of a saddle)'; hanbala (T.Y.K*) 'put a saddle on an animal, saddle, load' (denominative);
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. hanbal 'old and worn-out fur, garment' (for more details see Noeldeke 1910:54). There is the likelihood of a borrowing, but it is difficult to decide whether Arabic borrowed it from Geez or the other way around. ETHIOPIC: Te. hanbalāt 'belly band of camel's sad-dle', hanbalāt 'saddle girth', Amh. hambāl, ambāl 'breastplate for a horse'.

meaning: = Amh. tef 'a very fine grain used in making the əngära-bread';
origin_explanation: see also henbeliyāč.

meaning: to be identified with hanbāl, with liquids l:r.
'scab, scabby eruption of the skin, (T.K.G) series of spots, spotted eruption';
origin_explanation: from habarbarə (see above), with inserted n.

ETHIOPIC: Te. hanbar, hanbər, hanbart 'navel' Tna. hanbarti, hanbārti, Amh.Arg. əmbart, hambartī (with ī), Gur. (Zway) əmbara perhaps related to Gaf. gumbara, Amh. gumbara; from Cushitic: Qua. gumbəra, qəm-bära, Kham. herbir. See also Cerulli 1936:254; Co-hen 1947:no. 123.
ETHIOPIC: perhaps Te. hanfāsa 'confuse'.
ETHIOPIC: Te. hanfāsa 'disperse'.

meaning: 'winnow, sift, clean grain by rubbing lightly'; tahanfaṣa, passive; hanfuṣ (K*), pass. part. of hanfaṣa;
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. əmfuče 'kernels that are light due to frost and blow away with chaff'. Dillmann 111 (followed by Conti Rossini 1923:466) derives G. hanfaṣa from nafaṣa 'be scattered' (see below) with an agglutinated h coming from h, a causative mor-pheme. Conti Rossini ib. also identifies it with Tna. hanfāṣa (with h) 'mix wheat and barley' given by Bassano 8. However, the form is given as hanfāṣā by Yohannas G.E. and the meaning 'mix wheat and barley' is not to be identified with G. hanfaṣa 'win-now'. See also hanfaṣa II.
Dillmann wrongly makes a distinction between hanfaṭa (col.111) 'scratch, scrape' and hanfaṭa (col.628) 'strike, engrave'. In fact, the root is perhaps rather hanfaṭa; see below, and hfṭ.

meaning: 'clean grain by rub-bing lightly, winnow with a sieve';
origin_explanation: related to hanfaṣa, above.
ETHIOPIC: Tna. hangādā 'be refractory (cow that refuses to be milked)', Te. hangād 'that gives little milk'.
SEMITIC: Dillmann 109 connects it with Ar. hağğāz 'surround' which could apply to the eyebrows or to pubic hair. See also hagaz.

meaning: 'be anxious, be under stress, (T.Y.Gr.53, M) be ashamed, blush, (Gr.53) be sad, (M) suffocate, strangle'; yəhanəka (Gr.53), causative; hanik (T) 'shy'; (rather 'shyness'?); hanuk (Gr.53) 'who is ashamed, who blushes, who is sad'; hankat 'anguish, vexation, torment, (Gr.53) sorrow, grief, (Gr.53,T) shame, (Lt) cruel ferocity';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. hanka 'fear out of respect', hankātkāt 'fear' (denominative from hənkāt with reduplication of the last two radicals), Tna. hanakā 'be shy, timid, fear'.
SEMITIC: Ar. hanaka 'understand, have sound judg-ment', Heb. hanak 'train a person'; considered denominative from 'palate': Ar. hanak, Heb. hek.
see hənk, above.

meaning: 'munch, chew, eat'; yəhanəka (DTW), causative; tahanəka (DTW), passive; hanuk (DTW), pass. part. of hanaka; māhənak, pl. māhənak (K) 'jaw, means of chew-ing, means of grinding up meat and bones'; hanaka 'chew', from hanak 'palate', Soq. h-t-nk 'give food, receive food'.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. (tā)hannaka 'gulp down with an effort', Amh. annākā 'chew'.

hanaka (yəhnək) (K*)'initiate, inaugurate' (de-nominative from hənkā); transcription of Heb. hanukkā 'initiation, consecra-tion'. For 'initiation, consecration' from hanak 'pal-ate', see Landberg 1909:1712-4.
see hnks II.

meaning: 'kind of tree, fruit of that tree'.

'be lame, (T.Y.K) doubt, hesitate' (for the meanings, see also Amh. anəkkāsa, below); 'ahankasa (DTW 943), causative; hankās, (DTW 943) hankāsā 'lame'; hankāse 'limp (n.), lameness, (T) doubt' (n.); hankasənnā 'lameness, limp' (n.);
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. (Dat) hakas 'limp', Syr. haggīsā 'lame' (Dillmann 109; so also Brockelmann 1928:214). The connection between Ethiopic hnks and Heb. 'əqeş 'anklet, bangle' as suggested by Rundgren, Oriens 13-4 (1961),369 is unlikely. ETHIOPIC: Tna. hankasā 'be lame', Te. hankāsa, Amh. anəkkāsa 'limp, be slow-witted', Arg. anəkkāsa, Har. hankāsa 'lame', Gur. ankassa, passed into Cushitic: Bil.Sa.Af. hankas, Kham. ha-kes.
perhaps misprint for hankabənnā, and see hankaba 'camp'.
see also 'ankaso.
ETHIOPIC: perhaps Tna. hannənā 'apply oneself'.
SEMITIC: perhaps Ar. hanfā 'sea tortoise' (Dillmann 110).

meaning: 'be inclined, slip, slide, be prone (to any feeling), (Gr.52,Ll) be in anxiety, be fearful, fear, (K*,T,Y) be pampered, be capricious, (K*) desire something that is beyond one's capacity'; 'ahanqaqa (Gr.52) causative'; 'hanquq (L,K*,Gr.52) 'fearful, timid, (K*) pampered, capricious'; 'hanqäqe (D,T), hanq™äq™e (KG) 'inclination, being prone to (n.), being pampered, being capricious, (L,Lt.Gr.52) anxiety, fear'; 'mähanqaqi (Gr.52) 'who frightens'.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. hanqäqä 'be spoiled, be pampered', Te. hanqäqa 'live in luxury', hanquq 'spoiled'.
ETHIOPIC: Tna. ənqarti 'goiter', Amh. ənqart.

meaning: 'melting pot, furnace, pit in which blacksmiths melt iron';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. ənqärti 'shaft from which minerals or ores are taken'.

meaning: 'build up, construct, repair, erect'; 'ahnasa, causative'; 'tahansa 'be built, be erected'; 'hanäsi 'builder, mason, architect'; 'hənus 'built, constructed'; 'həns, hənsä, hənse- (with suffix pronouns) 'structure, building, construction, (Lt) rule'; 'hənsät 'construction, structure, process of build-ing'; '(ba'äla hansatä 'Feast of the Construction of the first church [the 20 and 21 of Sane']'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Praetorius, ZDMG 61 (1907), 616 consid-ers the verb hanasa 'build' a denominative from 'building', metathesis of Ar. hiṣn 'fortress' (for which see həṣn, below). ETHIOPIC: Tna. hanäsä 'build', Amh. annätä, Gur. anati 'carpenter' (from Amharic).

meaning: 'mouse'; variant of ənṣäwä, above. See also hanṣe.

origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Dillmann 79, 109 considers it a variant of hamet; cp. Tna. hannähännä 'rivalry'; in Be. hane 'reproach'.

meaning: 'cell, vault, slaughter house, (T) tavern, inn'; '(beta hanot 'tavern');
origin_explanation: Of Aram.-Syr. (hänūṭā, hānūṭā) origin (Fraenkel 172; Noeldeke 1910:45); also in Ar. hānūt, Heb. hānūt, Amh. hanot 'butcher shop'.

meaning: 'perfume the corpse, embalm the corpse, massage, rub with perfume';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. hannata 'embalm', Heb. hānāṭ, Syr. honat.
SEMITIC: Ar. hataba 'prune (a vine)'; the Ethiopic root is augmented by n. ETHIOPIC: Te. hantäba 'cut the ear of corn'.

SEMITIC: Ar. hatta'a 'cut, cleave, engrave', PBh. hāṭat 'dig out, cut', Aram. hāṭat, Syr. hat (htt!) 'dig, dig out', Md. htt (probably) 'dig out, excavate', Akk. haṭāṭu. ETHIOPIC: Tna. hanṭäṭä 'scratch', Te. hanṭäṭä 'be torn', Amh. anäṭäṭäq 'scrape, smooth by scraping'. The Ethiopic root is enlarged by n in relation to Semitic hṭṭ, hṭṭ. See also ənṭaṭa, hanṣaṣa.

meaning: 'come out of the nose (drink or food)';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. anäzäzä 'come out of the food (or drink) owing to a spasm of the trachea'. See also hazaza.
ETHIOPIC: Tna. hanzi'e 'beetle', Amh. ənzaz.
wrongly reconstructed from heppo diyaqon (see hippodiyäqon)

meaning: 'silken veil, cloth of silk and gold thread, colored cloth';
origin_explanation: to be identified with hippoditen, above.

meaning: 'the eastern door of the church, gateway of the church compound';
origin_explanation: possibly to be identified with Gk. (he)pylē 'the gate'. See also 'appälippu.
would it be the same as 'eppoppä?

SEMITIC: Ar. hawq 'loin', SAr. hqw 'loins, hips', Heb. heq 'lap'. ETHIOPIC: Te. haqqät 'loin, hip', Tna. haqqät, haqq™ä, haqq™e, Har. haqi 'back of body', coming to mean 'back of, behind, after' in the various Gurage dialects (see Leslau 1979:72, under anq™ä) as well as in Te. haqo [JNES 15(1956),241-2]. Littmann, ZS 1 (1922), 66 compares Har. haqi 'back' with Te. 'back'.

meaning: 'odoriferous wood, cedar wood, pine tree, (T) perfume, aroma';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Reinisch 1887:39 compares it with Tna.

meaning: 'hug, embrace, brood, fold (hands)'; 'ahqaqa, causative'; 'tahaqaqa, passive'; 'tahäqaqäfa 'embrace each other'; 'astahäqaqa, caus. of tahäqaqäfa'; 'haqäqi (K*), act. part. of haqaqa'; 'huquf, huquf 'embrace, hugged'; 'huqäfe 'embrace, embracing' (n); 'haqäfät 'embrace, guardianship'. 'mähaqäf (K*) 'means or place of holding in the arms';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Perhaps Ar. haqaqa 'be bent, be curved' (Ambros), Sh. hfeq 'embrace', and perhaps also hegef 'bend, sleep (protectively) over (a child)', Neo-Syr. hpaq 'embrace', Akk. epēqu, wohl 'umfassen' (von Soden 222); related to hbQ (with alternation of labials and metathesis): Soq. h-t-bq 'embrace one another', Heb. hābaq 'embrace', Aram. habbeq, Syr. habbaq 'surround, encompass', Md. hbQ 'embrace', Ug. hbQ. Hetzron, BSOAS 33 (1970), 612 suggests the possibility of Heb. 'qf 'surround'; note also Syr. 'apaq 'embrace' (Fraenkel, BA 3 [1898],73 against Barth 1893:25). Barth, ib. compares Ar. haqaq 'girdle, girth'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. haqäqä, haq™äfä 'embrace', Te. haqaqa, Arg. haqqäqä, Amh. aqäqäfä, Gur. anqäfä. Har. hafaqä (for haqaqäfa, with metathesis, a feature that is frequent in Harari). The root passed into Cushitic: Kam. hanqaffo, Sid. hanqafi, Sa. haqaq.

meaning: 'field, plain, desert, wilderness, countryside, district'; '(nəwäya haql 'weapon'); 'haqäla (K*) 'go to the field, (T,Y,M) become a rebel, plunder, become an uninhabited place'; 'haqälläqi 'countryman, farmer, native, rustic, (T) robber, fighter, barbarian, savage, (KG) one who lives in the desert'; 'haqälläwi 'same'; 'haqäli 'one of the field, peasant, rustic, rude, (Lt) rebel'; 'haqäläwi (Gr.50) 'uninhabited (place), empty, uncultivated (place)';

origin_explanation: SEMETIC: Ar. haqana 'churn'. ETHIOPIC: Te. (at)haqana 'churn', haqan 'buttermilk', Tna. haqana 'churn', haqan 'buttermilk'. See also aqan.

meaning: (T.M) 'level off, fasten, fix, (K*) make exact by increasing what is little or by diminishing what is much'
origin_explanation: SEMETIC: Ar. haqqa 'be suitable', haqq 'right, truth', SAr. haqq 'valid, binding', Soq. haq 'judgment', Heb. hoq 'prescription, rule, limit', Aram. haqq 'cut into > decree', Syr. huqqa 'rule', Md. haqq 'be orderly'. ETHIOPIC: Te. haqq 'right', Tna. haqq 'truth'. The root is related to hqq (see hagaqa, above); see also hqq, below.

haqqa 'a little, a little while, for a short time, a little way, by degrees'; (babbahaqq 'little by little, gradually, by degrees'); (əmdəhra haqqa 'soon after'); bahaqqu 'sufficiently, enough, very, very much, greatly, considerably, thoroughly, completely, exceedingly, utterly, intently, accurately, entirely, (Lt) in a little time'; (with a negative) 'not at all'. The basic meaning is 'be in the right measure (be it little or much)'; see haqaqa, above.

astahaqara, astahaqara, (K*) ahqara 'disdain, despise, look down on, deceive, esteem lightly, profane, humiliate, behave contemptuously, treat with contempt, vilify, revile, abuse, mock, ridicule, blame, censure'; tahaqara (K*), passive; haqoq (K*)r, pass. part. of astahaqara, also 'submissive, vile, despicable'; haq (M), haqr (K*) 'contempt, vileness, schism, heresy'; haqrat, haqrat 'dishonor, contempt, disdain'.
astahaqarot (Gr.50), part. of astahaqara; astahaqarot (Lt) 'insolence, dishonor, treachery'.

meaning: (Gr.50) haqya 'gnash the teeth, grind the teeth, break with the teeth, break by chewing, (M) grind one tooth against another, (T) bring close (probably, the teeth), chew hard food (such as roasted grain), (Lt) crunch'; ahqaya, causative; tahaqaya, passive; tahaqaya (K*) 'grind the teeth against each other'; haqayi (K*), act. part. of haqaya; haqay (K*) 'gnashed (teeth)'; haqe, haqay, (T) haqəy, haqəy "a, (L) haqiy 'gnashing of teeth'.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. haqaya 'gnash the teeth', Te. haqa (Reinisch, Gaf. aqqa (Polotsky, JAOS 69[1949],41), also in Cushitic: Bil. haqaq-d 'gnash the teeth', Kam. anqe-kko 'chew', Dar. unce. Or could Kam. anqe, unce be identified with Semitic hnk (see heka) with k:q:?

meaning: 'army, troops, hosts, soldiers, soldiery, captains, officers'
origin_explanation: harawi 'soldier, captain, freeborn, nobleman'; harawənna (Lt) 'freedom'; harənnat (T.M) 'freedom'. SEMITIC: Ar. hurr 'freeborn', SAr. hr 'free', Heb. hor, hor-im 'free men', PBh. ben hor-in 'free, freed', Aram. bar horin, Syr. hera, heraya, Md. br haria. ETHIOPIC: Amh. hara 'army, troops' (from Geez); harari 'army, band' (Littmann-Höfner 404). For te. haray 'free, freedom', cp. Tna. hara 'free', harənnat 'freedom', Gur. hurru bala 'be free'. While it is tempting to connect the meanings of hara 'army', harawi 'soldier, freeborn' and harənnat 'freedom', this may not be the case.

from Ar. harb; also in Te. harab, Har. harbi, Amh.Gur. harb, arb; Te. (tä)haraba 'fight' is denominative. SEMITIC: SAr hrb-t 'battle', Soq. harib 'fight', hereb 'sword', Aram.-Syr. harba, Md. harb, hrb.

meaning: = Amh. əmbay 'kind of plant having sour fruit'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: perhaps Te. haraba 'a plant'.

meaning: 'scrape, delete by scraping, tan (hide)'; could be either an original or a reconstructed perfect from the imperfect yəhərrab, from *harraba; herabi (T*); should be herabi, herəbi (K*) 'tanner'
origin_explanation: seems to be a wrongly reconstructed form of Amh. arraba 'make Moroccan leather', from *rb (with initial h instead of *).

meaning: 'fend off, parry, ward off a blow with a shield'

origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. harbätä 'not know how to distinguish between things'; perhaps also Amh. tä-rbätäbätä, from rbt (with reduplication of the last two radicals), from an original *rbt.

meaning: (L) harada 'slaughter, sever, cut the throat, slay, destroy'; ahərada (K*), causative; taharada, passive; taharada (K*) 'slaughter one another'; haradi (K*), act. part. of harada; hərud, pass. part. of harada; harədat, verbal noun of harada;
origin_explanation: SEMETIC: Ar. harada 'pierce'. G.R. Driver in Baumgartner 1967:54 connects this root with Heb. harag which occurs in several passages in the meaning 'set out, hurry' which he derives from the basic meaning 'pierce' (Ar. harada) > 'he separated himself from his own people' > 'hurried away on a journey, set out'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. harada 'slaughter', Te. hara, Amh.Gur. arrada, Arg. arrada; passed into Cushitic: Be. harad, Qua. ard.
seems to be a plural form.

meaning: (K) harasa 'grind, pulverize, reduce to powder, mash, crush, cut off'; aharada, causative; taharda, passive; haradi 'miller'; hərud 'ground, pulverized'; harid, (K.T) haris 'powder, flour, dough'; hərdat, verbal noun of harada; mäh(a)rad 'mill, millstone, lower millstone'; (mährada 'adg 'millstone', see adg); (əbna mährad 'millstone'); (beta mährad [L] 'mill');
origin_explanation: SEMETIC: Related to Ar. harasa 'split', SAr. hrd 'Mehlkuchen' (?) (Müller 1983:280). ETHIOPIC: Tna. haris 'flour', Arg. hart, Gur. art; passed into Cushitic: Sa.Af. harid.

meaning: (K.Y.M) 'drip, drop'; harafa, (KG) harifa, harafa 'saliva, phlegm, foam, (T.M) oil of mustard, juice (sap) of spinach'; harfot (M) 'juice of peas or chickpeas';
origin_explanation: SEMETIC: Related to Heb. arap 'drip, trickle'. ETHIOPIC: Amh. aräfa, aräfat 'foam', Gur. aräfa, Arg. aräfa, haräfat, Har. haräfa, horofa, and perhaps also Te.Tna. afra 'foam' (with metathesis and h:).

meaning: 'crush, grind, tear off a leaf from the top, make thin' (should be Amh. asassa rather than sassa 'be thin' as in T.Y); harifot (T) 'ground, crushed' (see also harifot);
origin_explanation: SEMETIC: Perhaps At. harafa (with h) 'pluck (a fruit), cut off (fruit)'. W. Müller suggests Ar. harafa 'turn away, discard from', Sh. haref 'remove'.
perhaps for harif with the meaning 'that which is harvested early', or 'that which is gathered'; cp. Ar. harafa 'pluck (a fruit), gather fruit'.
probably for harifot (see harafa II).
from Ar. hurāfā, pl. hurāfāt 'nonsense, gratuitous story' (Ambros).

meaning: 'wind around, interlace'; harag (pl. ahrag, ahrūg) 'vine shoot, twig, creeper, ornamented headings, ornamental design, vignette'; (nə'us harag 'dependent clause'); ('abiyy harag 'main clause'); expressions created by Marsə'e Hazān.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. harağa 'dense and tangled trees'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. harāg 'vine branch', Te. harāg 'a tree', Amh. arāg 'a creeping plant'; passed into Cushitic: Sa. harag, same.

meaning: 'ram, male of sheep or goats, kid (of goats)'.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Praetorius, ZDMG 43 (1899), 326 derives it from Cushitic: Sa.Af. hargi 'eunuch, castrated male sheep'; cp. also Som. urgi 'ram'.
ETHIOPIC: Tna. hargās 'crocodile'; augmented by r in relation to hagas, above.
see also harhar II.
perhaps to be connected with harhar I because of the many colors of a swampy place.

meaning: 'forbid, prohibit, abandon, set aside as sacred, declare sacred, consecrate, dedicate to a deity, serve a deity or a sacred thing'.
origin_explanation: harrama 'anathematize'; 'forbid, prohibit as illicit, consecrate, devote, cause to be declare as taboo, declare illicit, anathematize'; taharrama, taharrama 'be forbidden, abstain, keep oneself away from something (because of its being illicit), make a vow, be taboo, cultivate sacred things'; harāmi (K.Gr.46), act. part. of harama 'abstinent, penitent'; (harāme 'akl 'one who abstains from food'); harūm 'not permissible, illicit, consecrated, dedicated (to God), anathematized, who abstains'; harm 'forbidden thing, prohibited by setting aside as sacred'; harmat 'prohibition, abstinence, sacred rites, ritual, religion, ceremony, mortification, anathema'; ahrāmi, māhrami (Gr), part. of ahrāma 'sanctuary, shrine, temple, chapel, temple of the pagans'; taharāmi 'bereaved, who abstains, who keeps away (from something)'; taharmo 'abstinence'; taharəmt 'abstinence, vow, devotion, mortification'; SEMITIC: Ar. haruma 'be forbidden, be harrama 'forbid, pronounce inviolable or entitled to respect', Sar. harm 'prohibit', Sh. ahrim 'forbid', Heb. hrm (hif'il) 'banish' (for the various of Heb. hrm, see G.R. Driver in Baumgartner 1967:56-9), Aram. harem 'excommunicate, declare something set apart for the temple's use', Syr. hrm 'interdict, separate from the oracle, devote to God', Md. hrm 'ban, curse', Akk. harāmu 'separate'. For 'sanctuary', cp. Ar. (Dat) mihrām, Te. məhrām. As for Ar. mihrām 'prayer niche', Landberg 1920:396-8 derives it from Sar. mhrm, whereas R.S. Serjeant, BSOAS 22 (1959), 439-453 derives it from Sar. mhrb. See also Noeldeke 1910:52, n.3. ETHIOPIC: Te. harrāma 'forbid', Tna. harāmā 'be prohibited', Amh. harrāmā, arrāmā 'forbid, ban'. Note that the forms haram, harim 'forbidden, taboo' in the various Ethiopian languages are borrowed from Arabic.
metathesis for hartəmənnā; see hartama.
ETHIOPIC: Te.Tna. harmāz 'elephant'; passed into Cushitic: Af. harmaz, Qua. harmāz. See Hommel 333, 374, 381, and harmās, above.
from Amh. arāngə'ade.
is perhaps a loanword from Ar. hurr 'free', and is not to be connected with harā.

meaning: 'rebellious, be violent'; harapi (MA has harapi) 'violent, criminal'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Grimme 1914:261 connects it with hariba 'be violent, be vehemently angry', Heb. 'say sharp things', Syr. harrep 'sharpen'.

meaning: 'an insect, (K) which is nibbled, chewed, gnawed, bitten (food, fruit) one would expect 'which nibbles, chews, so on''
origin_explanation: from Ar. hurqūṣ 'winged insect'.

meaning: 'burn (intr.), be ablaze, be hot, be grilled, be dried up'; ahrara 'burn up (tr.), consume, dry up'; harrur 'burnt, dried out'; harur 'intense heat, ardor, fervor' (probably from Ar. harūr); harār 'ardor, fervor, heat, glowing ashes, (Gr.47) fever'; (Gr.47 has an entry harār and but the quoted text has harār harr);
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. harra 'be hot', Sh. har 'heat' (n.), Heb. harārā (hry) 'be hot', harhur 'feverish heat', Aram. harr 'burn', Syr. har 'be dry', Md. hr 'hot', Ug. hrr 'scorch, roast', Akk. erēru 'be and parched' (?), arāru 'burn up'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. harārā 'burn, be hot, be but Te. harra 'be hot', harrur 'heat' (n.), Har. harrā 'hot', Amh.Gur. arrārā 'be charred'; also in Cushitic: Tem. harrərre 'be hot', Sid. āri, Or. aru 'be charred', ār 'be angered', Bil. harār 'burn', Af. ur.
SEMITIC: Ar. harīr 'silk', Soq. haraher. ETHIOPIC: Te.Tna. harīr 'silk', Te. also Har. Amh. har, harir, probably from Ar. harīr; also in Cushitic: Sa. harīr, Af. harēr.

meaning: 'plow, cultivate land'; ahrasa, causative; taharasa, passive; harāsi (pl. harast, harāsəyān) 'plowman, farmer'; harəs (K*) 'plowed'; harəs (K*) 'plowed field, farmland'; harāsəwi 'plowman, farmer'; harsat (K*) 'act of plowing'; māhras (pl. mahāras) 'plowshare, plow'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. harata 'plow', Sar. m-hrt-t 'plowland', Heb. harāš 'plow', Syr. hərəf, Md. hrt 'dig out', Ph. hrš 'plow', Ug. hrt, Akk. erēšu 'cultivate a field'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. harāšā 'plow', Te. harsa, Har. harāsa, Arg. harrāsa, Amh.Gaf. arrāšā, Gur. arāsā. For 'plow' (n.), cp. Te.Tna. mahrāša, Amh.Gur. marāša. The root passed into Cushitic: Bil. arās, Sa.Af. harās.

meaning: 'practice sorcery, foretell the future, devise, (K*) have bad thoughts, (M) lie'; harāsi 'magician, who has the divining spirit, enchanter'; haras, hars, (L) harš, (KG) haris 'incantation, divining power, divination'; (sab'a harš [L], sab'a haris [Lt] 'diviners'); harso (T.M) 'one who predicts, magician'; harāsəwi, see harāsi.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Heb. 'hereš 'magic', Aram. harāš 'magician', harš-īn 'magic', Syr. harrāšā 'magician', Md. haršīn 'enchantment, magic', Ug. h-r-š 'work magic'. I Barth 1893:3 connects the root with Ar. sihr (with metathesis) 'magic' against Fraenkel, BA 3 (1898), 64. Von Soden 324 connects it with Akk. harāšu 'tie, bind', the tying referring to the magical practice of tying knots (see also habr II). On Heb. hereš and on its connection with Ar. hurs 'the food given to the women in childbed (as a magical drug)
ETHIOPIC: probably for harrasa 'be in childbed', and Cp. also Tna. harāsā 'sleep, slumber, be in childbed' (Tna. harāsā would then be for harāsā).
is there any connection with harast (pl. of harāsi) 'magicians' (for which see harasa II)?
ETHIOPIC: Te.Tna. haris 'rhinoceros', Amh. haris, awraris (from awra aris, originally awrā haris); passed into Arabic harīs (Noeldeke 1910:57), and Cushitic: Sa. haris. See also Hommel 332, 381.
ETHIOPIC: Tna. harāšā 'usury', harāšā 'lend money at interest', Amh. araja 'usury'.

meaning: 'become wretched, be abominable, be miserable, be distressed, look poor (due to hardship, illness, ignorance), be upset by grief or worry, be vile, be disgraced, be emaciated'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: The connection with Ar. (ta)hattama (with augmented r) 'crumble, break into small bits' suggested by Růžička 166 is plausible. See also harma-tannā.
from Gk. hairetikos (pl. hairetikoi) through Chr.-Ar. (pl.) harātiqa.

ETHIOPIC: Te. harāwya 'boar', Amh. harīyya 'wild pig', Gur. harāyā 'porcupine' (Selti) irāyā 'pig', also in Cushitic: Or. eriya, Af. hārāy, Sa. harā. Be. harāwya. Appleyard 43 from G. harawa 'dig', but the Geez root for 'dig' is harawa.

from Aram.-Syr. has, has l- (Noeldeke 1910:38). The expression also occurs in PBH. has wəšālōm 'let it not be, God forbid!', Md. has. Arabic hāšā, in hāšāka, hāšā laka 'God forbid' is not a loanword (as suggested by Noeldeke), but none of the other Semitic languages has s. The root is compared with Ar. hassa 'be compassionate' (Künstlinger, OLZ 33[1930].669-70). See also Rundgren, Oriens 13-14 (1960-61).368. ETHIOPIC: Te.Tna. hašša 'far be it!', from Ar. hāšā; also in Amh. šāša (reduplicated ša).

meaning: 'think, believe, impute, consider, estimate, esteem, appreciate, regard, deem worthy, take into consideration, have regard for'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. hasiba 'think', hasaba 'calculate, count', Soq. hoseb 'count', Heb. hāšab 'think', Aram. hāšeb, Syr. hāšab, Md. hšb, Ph. hšb. ETHIOPIC: Tna. hasābā 'think', Te. hasba, Arg. hassāba, Har. hāsāba, Amh. assābā, Gaf. issābā, Gur. assābā; passed into Cushitic: Sid. assabi, Bil. hasaba, Sa. Af. hasāb.
see hassit.

meaning: 'kind of worm, (Gr.49) caterpillar that appears during rains'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. hašāka 'a worm'.

meaning: 'to rain, be cold, make shiver (cold, rain)'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. hašaka 'let fall rain', hašāka 'abundant rain'. ETHIOPIC: Amh. assakk alā, aššakk alā 'shiver from cold'.

meaning: 'put on a halter or a headstall on a mule or a horse, lead by the bridle'

origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. hasala 'urge on a beast', Akk. ašlu 'rope' > Aram. ašlā (Zimmern 35, Kaufman 39), Syr. išlā, Md. ašlā. Or could G. hasala be connected with Syr. hsal 'restrain'? ETHIOPIC: Te. hasla, hasla 'bridle' (v.), hasal, hasāl 'camel's bridle'.
SEMITIC: Ug. hsn 'grasshopper' (Fisher 88), Dahood 1965:58 compares Heb. hāšil with Ug. hsn (with alternance l:n). ETHIOPIC: Tna. hasān 'termite', Amh. asān 'termite, small locust'.

meaning: 'cover, hide, put (into the pocket), (KG) intertwine, put something around the neck'

(T) ḥasama 'be bad, be evil, be foul, be deformed, be ugly'; 'aḥəšama 'make foul, make evil, bring into disgrace, ill-treat, treat badly, do evil, corrupt, act wickedly, afflict, do harm, hurt, damage, abuse, offend, curse'; ('aḥəšama gəṣṣo lā-lehu 'he looked at him unpleasantly'); ('aḥəšama qālā lā-le 'he spoke evil against'); 'astaḥašama 'consider disgraceful, consider hateful, consider ugly';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. ḥašima 'be confounded by shame', 'put to shame, annoy, anger'. Šḥ. ḥašəm 'unpleasant taste in one's mouth'. Praetorius, ZDMG 62 (1908). 750-1 (so also Barth 1893:54) connects the Geez root with Ar. ğāšim 'ignorant, dumb, stupid' and suggests that the Geez root is an original ḥašama. However, Geez ḥ: Ar. ğ is not a normal correspondence. Fraenkel 1898:62, and Praetorius, ib. rightly object to the comparison with Heb. ḥāmas 'treat violently' suggested by Barth 1893:54. ETHIOPIC: Tna. ḥašəmā 'be bad, be vicious', Amh. ḥašəmāt 'ugliness' (from Geez).

meaning: 'detestable, abominable, disgraceful, despicable, perverted, ugly, evil, foul, bad, wicked, indecent, abusive, harmful'; (ba-ḥašum, ḥašumā 'wretchedly, wickedly'); ḥašumāt 'abominations'; ḥašəm 'ugliness, blemish, guilt, abomination, wickedness, villainy, evil, evil deed, misdeed, harm, infamy, disgrace'; (ḥašəma rā'y (Lt) 'ugliness'); ḥašəm (TS), same as ḥašəm; ḥašmat 'ugliness, trouble, defect, blemish, fault'; 'aḥšāmi, māḥšami (Gr.46), act. part. of 'aḥšama;
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. ḥašima 'be confounded by shame', 'put to shame, annoy, anger'. Šḥ. ḥašəm 'unpleasant taste in one's mouth'. Praetorius, ZDMG 62 (1908). 750-1 (so also Barth 1893:54) connects the Geez root with Ar. ğāšim 'ignorant, dumb, stupid' and suggests that the Geez root is an original ḥašama. However, Geez ḥ: Ar. ğ is not a normal correspondence. Fraenkel 1898:62, and Praetorius, ib. rightly object to the comparison with Heb. ḥāmas 'treat violently' suggested by Barth 1893:54. ETHIOPIC: Tna. ḥašəmā 'be bad, be vicious', Amh. ḥašəmāt 'ugliness' (from Geez).

meaning: 'straw, piece of straw, dry reeds, stubble, dry twigs, refuse, chaff'; ḥašara (K*) 'collect straw or chaff, peel fruit' (denominative);
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. ḥušr, ḥušur 'husks'. Soq. ḥošir 'bad pasture'. Bittner 1909:18 compares Mh. ğəšēr 'straw'. ETHIOPIC: Te. ḥašār 'straw'. Tna. ḥasər, Amh. əsār, (from Geez) 'dregs, husk', Gur. əšāra, Har. ḥaššār; passed into Cushitic: Kam.Qab. əšara, Bil. ḥažžār.

meaning: 'make happy, give joy'; ḥasaya, same; taḥasaya 'rejoice, enjoy oneself, joyful, exult, be glad'; ḥassət 'joy, gladness, happiness'; ḥasuy (Gr.46) 'joyful, pleased, satisfied, content, glad'; taḥassət (M) 'joy, happiness, exultation';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. (Yem) ḥseh 'bravo!', Akk. ḥašāšu 'h) 'rejoice'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. əssəy 'bravo!', Amh. əššəy, exclamation of joy, əsso, əššo 'bravo!', ḥassət 'joy' (from Geez), Gur. əššu 'bravo!'; also in Cushitic: Had. ḥašu, Kam. əššu.

SEMITIC: Ar. ḥazwa, ḥuzwa 'arrow', Sar. ḥəzəw 'archer', Heb. ḥeṣ 'arrow', Aram. ḥeṣ, Ph. ḥṣ, Ug. ḥṣ. Akk. iṣṣu, uṣṣu. See also Noeldeke 1910:147-8. ETHIOPIC: Te. ḥas 'sting', Har. ḥināč 'arrow', mann, ZS 1[1922].57), Amh. ḥaṣ (from Geez).

SEMITIC: Ar. ḥiṣn 'fortress', Sar. m-ḥṣn 'defense work', Soq. ḥoṣon 'fortress'. Praetorius, ZDMG 61 (1907). 616 connects G. ḥəṣən 'fortress' with ḥanasa 'build' (see above). Brockelmann 1928:247 compares Syr. ḥeṣn 'be strong', ḥeṣnā 'fortress'. Ar. ḥaṣuna 'be strong', ḥiṣn 'fortress'. As for ḥiṣn, Fraenkel 235 (following Guidi) considers it a loanword coming from Syr. ḥeṣnā. He explains ḥiṣn (with ṣ) against Syr. ḥeṣnā (with s) as a folk etymology due to Ar. ḥaṣuna 'be inaccessible'. Aram.-Syr. origin of Ar. ḥiṣn is doubtful since ḥṣn also occurs in South Arabic. See also Sandberg 1920:424ff.

meaning: (T) ḥaṣara 'fence off, enclose with a fence, make a rampart, build a mound, compass, encompass, besiege'; 'aḥṣara, causative; also 'besiege'; taḥaṣara, passive; taḥaṣara, reciprocal of ḥaṣara; ḥaṣāri, act. part. of ḥaṣara; ḥaṣur 'fenced-in, besieged, surrounded'; ḥaṣur 'fenced-in place, fence, wall, hedge, enclosure, rampart, mound, trench'; (ḥaṣurā masqal 'Rampart of the Cross', a group of short poems for the days of the week; see Lifschitz 87 ff); ḥaṣrat (Gr.67) verbal noun of ḥaṣara; məḥəṣār (Gr.67) 'place where one makes a fence';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. ḥaṣīra, ḥiṣār 'enclosure', Ar. (Dat) ḥaḍīra (see Landberg 1920:432ff), Sar. ḥṣwr (pl.) 'enclosed cultivated lands', Šh. ḥoṣor 'besiege, hem in', Heb. ḥāṣēr 'yard, enclosure' (see Orlinsky, JAOS 26 [1939].22ff), Aram. ḥuṭrā, Syr. ḥəṣārā, Ph. ḥṣr, Ug. ḥṭr, ḥzr 'court'. possibly Akk. ḥašūru (with ḥ) 'enclosure for sheep'. Note that Akk. uṣurtu (Orlinsky, ib. 24) is not given in von Soden 1440 in the meaning of 'enclosure'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. ḥaṣārā 'make a fence', Te. ḥaṣṣāra, Arg. ḥaṭṭāra, Har. ḥēṭāra, Amh. aṭṭārā, Gur. aṭārā.

meaning: (T) ḥaṣaṣa 'be deficient, subside, wane, decrease (intr.), be wanting, be less, be subtracted, diminish (intr.), be inferior (in quality), be short of, be the least, be little, be imperfect'; ḥaṣaṣa (T) 'shorten'; (zayəḥaṣṣəṣ 'imperfect'); 'aḥṣaṣa 'decrease, detract, subtract, diminish, reduce, lessen, abate, make less, make few, omit, make inferior, slight, belittle'; ('aḥṣaṣa rə'so 'he humiliated himself'); taḥaṣaṣa (Lt), passive of 'aḥṣaṣa; ḥaṣuṣ 'diminished, minor, less, insufficient, lacking in fullness, wanting, deficient, inferior, low, the least, small, short, imperfect, dwarf'; (warḥ ḥaṣuṣ 'hollow month'; Neugebauer 79, n. 1); ḥaṣuṣān (also zenā mawā'əl) 'Chronicles (Bible), genealogy, lineage, generations'; (ḥaṣuṣān maṣḥaf 'Chronicles'); (ḥaṣuṣānā nagaśt 'Paralipomena'); ḥaṣaṣ 'lack, deficiency, decrease, subtraction, scarcity, shortage, loss, waning (n.), inferiority, diminution, reduction, want, imperfection'; ḥuṣāṣe 'decrease, diminution'; ḥaṣṣat, same as ḥaṣaṣ;
'decrease, deficiency, inferior condition, diminution, waning'; täḥṣäṣe, täḥṣäṣe, same; täḥṣäṣit 'scarcity, littleness'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. ḥaṣṣa 'cut off', PBh. ḥäṣaṣ, Akk. ḥuṣṣu su (with ḥ) 'cut, break'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. ḥäṣäṣä 'be little, be deficient', 'äḥṣäṣä 'diminish', Amh. 'äṭṭäṭä, 'aṣṣäṣä 'lack, be deficient, diminish', ḥäṣäṣ 'decrease' (from Geez); also in Cushitic: Bil. ḥaṣaṣ 'cut in small pieces'. See also ḥanṣäṣa.

meaning: 'loneliness, helplessness' possibly a secondary form of ḥṣṣ, above.
origin_explanation: possibly a secondary form of ḥṣṣ, above.
from Ar. ('al)-ḥūt (for which see Kunitzsch 1961, Index, p. 124).

meaning: 'bind with leather strips, fasten the framework of the roof with leather strips'
'third Coptic month, from October 28 to November 27'
origin_explanation: from Coptic (Saidic) ḥatōr, also Chr.-Ar. ḥatūr; see also 'atur.

meaning: 'search out, inquire, question, ask, interrogate a witness, investigate, explore, examine, beseech, scrutinize, discern, adjudicate'; 'aḥtata, causative; taḥatata, (K*) taḥatta, passive; taḥatata (K*) 'investigate one another'; 'astaḥatata 'cause to search, investigate'; ḥatati, act. part. of ḥatata; ḥatätä 'searching, scrutiny, question, judicial investigation, request, inquiry, inquisition, trial, commentary'; ḥatit (Lt) 'inquiry'; ḥatato (M) 'request'
'mark, (T.M) abject, base' transcription of Heb. ḥattāw 'the mark'. The meanings 'abject, base' are undoubtedly pretations given by Tayyä to the noun ḥetyot.
origin_explanation: transcription of Heb. ḥattāw 'the mark'. The meanings 'abject, base' are undoubtedly pretations given by Tayyä to the noun ḥetyot.

meaning: 'cut (wood), split (wood), gather (wood)'; 'aḥtaba (Gr.66), causative; taḥataba (Gr.66), passive; ḥatäbi, ḥatṭäb 'woodcutter, one who (wood)'; ḥatub, pass. part. of ḥataba; ḥatb, (M) ḥaṭb 'wood that is cut, wood gathered'; ḥatbat (Gr.66) 'act of cutting or (wood)'; mäḥṭäb (Gr.66) 'place where one cuts or wood'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. ḥataba 'gather wood', ḥaṭab 'firewood', Soq. ḥaṭab 'gather wood', Heb. ḥaṭab 'gather fire wood', PBh. ḥāṭāb 'cut, split', Ug. ḥṭb 'firewood'. ETHIOPIC: Te. ḥaṭaba 'gather wood'.
ETHIOPIC: Tna. ḥawi 'fire', Te. ḥaw, Amh. ḥaya 'burn'; also in Cushitic: Bil.Kham. ḥaū y 'burn' (intr.). See also ḥawaya.
from Ar. 'al-ḥāwī 'that collects' (see E. Hammer- Schmidt 1977:127).
it is a plural form.

meaning: 'mix mingle'; ṭawala (K), taḥawala (D), taḥawala (L) 'be model, be mingled, be united'; taḥawala (K.M.); (Dillmann has ta- ḥawala) 'be associated (with), participate, have communion (with), have fellowship (with have dealings (with someone)'; ḥawäli (K*), act. part. of ḥawala; ḥawäwəl 'connected, united, associated, min- gled'; (ḥawawəla (L) 'jointly, together')
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. (tä)ḥawäla 'eat and drink in commu- nity', ḥawäla 'common eating and drinking'. also ḥawlayya II.

meaning: 'turn around' probably reconstructed from ḥawalt, below.
origin_explanation: probably reconstructed from ḥawalt, below.

meaning: 'sharp obsidian' K quotes ḥawlät from Job 41:21, but Asmara Bible edition has ḥawält ḥw-A-T. which see below.
origin_explanation: K quotes ḥawlät from Job 41:21, but Asmara Bible edition has ḥawält ḥw-A-T. which see below.

SEMITIC: Ar. ḥawla 'around', SAr. ḥwl, Soq. 'go around'; perhaps Heb. ḥāl (ḥwl) 'dance round dances or hel 'rampart', both roots possibly go back to 'be round, do something in circular me- ments'. For 'calendar', cp. Ar. ḥawl 'year'. See 60ff. Rabin, Orientalia 32 (1963).120, n. 3 connects the Geez root with the SAr. (Minaean) architectural term ḥyl-n. ETHIOPIC: Tna. ḥawalti 'column', Te. ḥawəlt, Amh. ḥawəlt. Geez ḥawəlt 'calendar' also derives from basic meaning of the months going around in year.

meaning: 'take a rest' ḥawänit (T) 'resting place' (perhaps for ḥa nit, below).

'go, go forth, proceed, depart, follow a way of life, have sexual intercourse'; (zäyäḥawwər 'a moving creature'); 'aḥora, causative; also 'lead, bring (into)'; taḥawra, (K*) taḥora 'go, walk'; 'astaḥawra, (K*) 'astaḥawära 'bring back, re- port to, (Lt) send (lit. 'make go'), (KG) help to carry'; ḥawäri (pl. ḥawäräyan), act. part. of ḥora; also 'traveler'; (ḥawäre mäy 'water carrier'); (ḥawäre bə'əsi (Gr.56) 'man who copulates with a man, sodomite'); (ḥawäre 'ənəṣäṣä (Gr.56) 'man who copulates with animals'); ḥawwər (K*) 'who walks'; ḥawär (pl. ḥawärät) 'colonnade, platform, pub- lic place, courtyard, porch, chamber, alcove, storeroom'; (ba-ḥawär-ä 'in the compass of'); ḥawäräyä (pl. ḥawäräyan, ḥawäräyät) 'traveler, messenger, envoy, apostle, (D*) Acts of Apos- tles'; (gädla ḥawäräyät 'the Lives of the Apostles'; for a bibliography, see Hammerschmidt 1973: 138; Strelcyn 1974:9); ḥawäräyäwi 'apostolic'; ḥurat 'course, walking, going, manner of going, step, journey, departure, way of life, manners, customs, conduct, behavior'; ḥuratät (pl. of ḥurat) 'behavior, way of life, manners'

origin_explanation: metathesis, Sar. hwr 'settle in a town' > 'go'. Dillmann 113 compares G. hora with Ar. hāra 'return', a comparison not accepted by Noeldeke 1910:48 (see also Landberg 1923:1506). Arabic ha wārayyūna 'apostles' is borrowed from G. hawārayā (already recognized by Ludolf 637). ETHIOPIC: Tna. horā 'go', Gaf. (a)horā, Amh. horā 'in huru bāsālam 'go in peace!', a benediction said at the end of the communion (from Geez). Har. hāra 'go'. Gur. wārā. The initial h of the root hora (hwr) reappears in Gur. (Endegeñ) yax'ār. In Tigre the root appears in hawarya 'apostle'. For G. hawār, cp. also Amh. hawār 'series of terraces, area around a town' (from Geez).

meaning: (T.Y.M) 'move (intr), shake (intr), wag, agitate, mix'; 'ahosa 'move, cause to move, shake (one's head), agitate, stir up, toss'; tahawasa, tahawasa, tahosa, pahosə 'also 'move (intr.), creep'; (zayəthawwas 'reptile'); hawāsi (Gr.57), act. part. of hosa; hawwəs, hawwəs (Lt.K), hawwəs (L) 'agitated, shaken'; hawəs, see below; husat 'movement, motion, moving (n.), agita tion'; tahawsə 'movement'; tahawāsi 'that which stirs, that which moves, reptile, movable property'; tahawāsāyən 'crawling beings'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. hāsa (hws) 'prowl about' (for hws, see Landberg 1920:512f), Ar. (Dat) haswās 'agitation'. Barth 1893:68 also compares Ar. hatta 'urge' (see also Noeldeke 1904:44). Heb. hāš (hwš) 'get excit ed, hurry' (Driver, JTS 32[1931]:253, Eitan, HUCA 12-13[1937-8]:72). Syr. hathet 'excite' (Barth 1893:68). Ug. hš 'hurry!'. Akk. hāšu, hiāšu 'hurry, shake'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. hasosa 'move', Tna. hawwəsā 'mix'. Amh. (h)awwāsā 'mix, stir', Har. hēwāsa 'stir'.

meaning: explanation of admiration, of joy, or of approval, bravo!; (hawesā lamas'atkəmu habena 'welcome!, your coming to us is welcome');
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. ašša, exclamation of pleasure.

meaning: 'physical sense, mem ber of the body'.
origin_explanation: From Ar. hawāss (pl. of hāssa); also Amh. hawwās.

meaning: (T.M) 'shine, light up, beco me (K) be pure, be clear, be bright'; undoubtedly to be identified with hawwasa below.

(T) hawwada 'glance, look on, look after, peep, visit, pa watch, observe, explore, inspect, ove care of, be solicitous, (K.H) spy'; (zayəhewwəs 'watchman'); tahawwasa, passive; 'astahawwasa (Gr.58) 'consider, medi der'; hawwāsi, act. part. of hawwasa; also 'overseer, watcher, watchman, spy'; hawwus, pass. part. of hawwasa; hawwāsā 'survey'; hawwāsā 'visit'; hawwāse 'watching, contemplation, v visit, inspection, providence'; hawwasot (Lt) 'visit, providence'; mahawwəs 'spectator, observer, (K*)' mahawwəs 'view, observatory, place which one looks (such as window), visitation'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. hāwasa (with h) 'consider, (Dozy), hashaşa 'appear in broad light', t hs related to hws (V. Christian, 54[1957].266). ETHIOPIC: Tna. hawwāsā 'appear'. Tna. haw around, inspect'. Amh. awwātā 'visit' (An form of hawwāsā). See also hayāṣa.

meaning: (T.Y) 'become dark -> to sunset), become gloomy, become eve ning'; hawāy, hawāy 'evening, the red glow the evening sky, twilight'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. hawīya < hawīwa, in ihwāwā red inclining to blackness'. Jeffery 286 considers Ar. hawīya, one of the names of hell, of Surah 101:6, as coming from G. hawāy and haw 'fire'. CUSHITIC: Sa. hawā 'sunset', Be. awi 'dusk, twilight'. For the red color, cp. perhaps haw 'fire', above.

meaning: (T) 'Oh!'; if it is an interjection expressing pain, cp. Gur. (Leslau 1979:639, under wa II).

(K*) 'be pleasant, b pleasing, be agreeable'; with object suffix pro nouns 'feel comfortable, be satisfied, agreea i-hawaza- with object suffix pronouns 'be pleased'; hawwaza, same as hawaza; also 'make pleas ant, gladden, give joy, delight, please'; 'ahoza same as hawwaza; also causative hawwaza; tahawwaza, (K*) tahawwaza 'take pleasure, t delight, delight oneself, be pleased'; 'astahawwaza 'make something pleasant'; 'astahawwaza 'find someth pleasant, regard as acceptable, enjoy, pleas delight'; hawz 'pleasure, delight, satisfaction, enj ment'; DTW 991 renders G. hawz with Amh. awza 'chat (Catha edulis)'; it is boiled to pro duce an infusion; this infusion is drunk cure gonorrhea; hawiz (Gr.57) 'agreeable, charming, pleasan hawāz 'pleasant, pleasing, delightful, agreea suave, sweet pleasure, delight'; 'astahawzə 'feeling of pleasure, pleasantne'.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. awaz 'attractive, tasty'; probab also Te. hawwāza 'become fat', Tna. hawwāz 'increase (family)', that is, 'become pleasing, become enjoyable'.

meaning: Amh. 'step aside, get out of the way'; is perhaps a misprint for fāgagg alā 'smile', and could be identified with hawaza I.

'draw water'; M wrongly interprets Amh. tāllāqā (translation given by Tayyā for heba) as 'be deep' where in fact tāllāqā also means 'draw water with a container'; 'aheba (Gr.61), causative; tahayba (Gr.61), passive; hayābi (Gr.61), act. part. of heba; (hayābe ma'ār (Lt) 'one who receives the hon that is brought as tribute'); hayyub (Gr.61) 'drawn'.

origin_explanation: From Amh. ayb rendered in a Geez form.
see also haydanaya.
see also haydana.

meaning: (T) heka 'chew, masticate, (Gr.62) bite'; tahayka, passive; hayāki (Gr.62), act. part. of heka; hayyuk (Gr.62), pass. part. of heka; hikat (Gr.62, K*) 'act of chewing'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: related to the root hnk, hkk: Ar. hanak 'palate', Šh honuk 'the gum and the palate', Heb. hek 'palate', Aram. hikkā, ancient Aram. hnk, Syr. hekkā, hekkā, Md. hinkā. ETHIOPIC: Tna. hekā, hayākā 'chew', Te. hanāk 'palate', (tā) hannākā 'gulp down with effort', Har. heka 'chew', Amh. ənnākā (from hnk with n becom ing palatalized), Arg. hannāha (with h < k), Gaf. hannāhā, Gur. enke; also in Cushitic: Be. ayuk 'chew'. See also hanaka iv.

meaning: perceive, view, discern, look at, look on, observe, observe closely, consider carefully, search for, watch, examine, spy, explore, scrutinize, lie in wait, be on the lookout for'
origin_explanation: tahayāsa (Lt) 'watch'; hayāsi (K*), act. part. of astahāyāsa; hayyāse, huyāse, (KG) hayyāde 'observation, observing, lookout, being (n) viewed or seen'; mahayyas (K*) 'who perceives, who discerns'; mastahāyyas, (Gr.63, K*) 'astahāyāsi, act. part. of astahāyāsa; also 'explorer, examiner, curious person'; see also hawwaṣa.

meaning: wander about aimlessly, guard here and there'
origin_explanation: hāyat (K*) 'visiting, watching over'; the verb hayata seems to be a denominative from hāyat. As for hāyat, it is derived from wahaya, below.

meaning: live, be alive, come back to life, revive (intr.), be well, be healed, be cured, recover, be restored, be saved'
