origin_explanation: (haywa ʔamqatil 'he escaped death'); ʔahayawa 'keep alive, make to live, let live, enliven, vivify, raise up alive, restore to life, cure, heal'; astahāyawa 'bring back to life, repair, restore'; hayāw (fem. hayāwī) 'alive, living, healed, whole (unharmed), safe and sound'; with suffix pro-nouns: hayāwīnahomu 'they (who are) alive'; (hayāw nagus 'long live the king!'); (used in oaths, e.g. hayāw ʔəgzīʔabəḥer kama 'as God lives'; hayāw ʔana kama 'as I live'); ʔəg°ʔala ʔəmahāyāw 'children', lit. 'the mother of the living', that is, Eve; hayāw (Lt) 'healthy, sound'; haywat, hiwat 'life, lifetime, healing, health, salvation, restoration, state, (nafsa haywat 'living creature'); (used in oaths, e.g. haywata faraʔon kama 'by the life of Pharaoh'); hayāwənnā, (Gr.62) hayəwənnā 'state (condition) of living, of being alive'; māḥayaw, māḥayawi 'life-giving, saving, savior'; məḥ(ə)yāw (K*) 'place where one lives'; SEMITIC: Ar. hayya 'live'; SAr. hyw, Soq. hyy, Mh. hewya 'recover, be cured'. Heb. hāyā(h) 'live', Aram. hāyā, hāyī, Syr. hayyā, Md. hia, Ph. hyy. ETHIOPIC: Tna. hayāwəʔa 'live, be healthy' (also hawāyəʔa, with metathesis), Te. haya 'live', Tna. haya Te. hayot, Amh. haywāt, Gur. xəyāwāt; ʔaḥayawəʔa 'living, alive' (from Geez); passed into Cushitic: hay 'live', Sa.Af. hay.
probably from wəhəza 'flow', below.

ḥazʔat, (K*) ḥozʔat 'hiding place, den, lair, retreat, enclosure, manger, refuge, closed-in pasture, sheepfold'; SEMITIC: Ar. ḥazaʔa 'gather and drive camels'. ETHIOPIC: Te. ḥazʔat 'reserved pasture'. Tna. ḥazəʔə 'reserved pasture', Amh. ḥazʔat 'den, lair' (from Geez); passed into Cushitic: Bil. hedʔat 'robbers' den', Be. hidəwa (with alternance d:z).

meaning: observe one another, suspect one another'
origin_explanation: ʔəstahazzaba 'raise suspicion, (T) cause to be embittered toward each other'; ḥazābi (Gr.59), act. part. of hazaba; ḥazub (K), pass. part. of hazaba; hazbāt (D 1427) 'suspicion, expectation, opinion'; təḥzəbt 'thought, opinion, belief, imagination, conscience, doubt, suspicion, distrust, blame, fear of things to come, expectation, apprehension'. The opposite meanings of hazaba and of some derived forms seem to go back to the general idea of thinking or considering things from various points of view. SEMITIC: Ar. (ta)ḥazzaba 'plot, conspire'. Barth 1893:50 connects the root with Semitic hsb 'think' so also Noeldeke 1910:59, n. 3. ETHIOPIC: Te. (tā)ḥazzaba 'doubt, cast suspicion on', Amh.Gur. (tā)ḥazzaba, Har. (tā)ḥazzaba 'keep memory something that someone has done'; related to Tna. (tā)ʔazzaba.

SEMITIC: perhaps Ar. hazama 'tie up', with alternance of labials b:m (G. Salinger). ETHIOPIC: Amh. ʔəzābā 'plait, make thongs', Tna. ḥazābā 'interlace the braid of blue silk which Christians wear around their necks', Tna. ḥazab 'rein', Amh. ʔəzab, zab, Arg. zab, Gur. ʔəzab, zab; also in Cushitic: Kam. zaba, Qab.Al. ʔəzāba.

meaning: people, nation, Tna. hazbi, Amh.Gur. hazb; passed into Cushitic: Bil. hizb, Sa. hizb-e. Ambross connects G. hazb, with reservations, with hizb II.
origin_explanation: *hasbaka > hazbaka); so also Noeldeke 1910:59, n.3. ETHIOPIC: Te. hazb 'people, nation', Tna. hazbi, Amh.Gur. hazb; passed into Cushitic: Bil. hizb, Sa. hizb-e. Ambross connects G. hazb, with reservations, with hizb II.

meaning: be come a swamp, be swampy'
origin_explanation: ḥazhaz, (Lt) ḥazhaz, (T) ḥazhaz 'flint, gravel, place full of gravel, swamp, pool, muddy place, clod'; SEMITIC: Ar. ḥazīz 'place abounding with stones' (Dillmann 120). See also ḥazaza.

meaning: carry on the back, carry a burden'
origin_explanation: tahazala (K*), causative; tahazala, passive; tahazāla (K*) 'carry one another'; ʔəstahazāla (T.K*) 'cause to carry one another'; ḥazāli 'porter'; ḥazūl 'carried'; ḥazlat (Gr.58) 'act of carrying'; māḥzəl (K*.Gr.58) 'means of carrying'; ETHIOPIC: Tna. ḥazlā, ḥazlālā 'carry on the back', Te. ḥazla, Arg. ḥazlāla, Har. ḥazlā, Amh. ʔəzlā, Gur. ʔəzzālā.
see ʔəzl.

meaning: kind of game played with six sugar canes split in half'
origin_explanation: (called in Amh. ʔab). Guidi 1899:485, n. 5 derives it from Ar. hazl 'joke, jest' restricted in the translation of the Fāṭḥa nagast to a game. The derivation from Ar. ʔal-ḥādil 'ab-staining from' suggested by Dillmann 1399 is less likely.

meaning: be sad, be sorrowful, be grieved, be in mourning, have compassion, be sorry for, sympathize'
origin_explanation: ḥazzana, ḥazana (possibly with intensity); ʔaḥzana 'sadden, make sad, afflict, cause sorrow, inspire compassion'; ʔaḥzana with indirect object suffix pronouns, 'care for, be saddened for, be sorrowful for';
from Ar. hirz (pl. hurūz) 'amulet' (with metathesis). W. Müller suggests, with reservations, Ar. hadar 'precaution'.
either directly from Syr. haziran or through Chris-tian-Arabic haziran. See also aziran.

meaning: bench made of earth or stone, platform for sitting or sleeping

cough, (Y) swallow a liquid that chokes and causes coughing'; 'ahzaza, (T) 'ahzaza 'cause to cough, irritate, render rough, block the breath (from an origi-nal meaning 'feel discomfort in the throat, have a feeling of roughness'), (T) come out of the nose (food) while one is asleep, swallow a liquid that chokes and causes coughing, (K*) have heartburn'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. hazāz 'rough'. ETHIOPIC: Amh. anāzāzā 'come out of the nose (food or drink) because of a spasm in the trachea' is connected by Praetorius 1879:100 with G. hzz augmented by n. See also hanzaza.
